query:
You are working on the Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime repository.
The codebase is at version 0.6 (commit: e721269c).

## Issue to Resolve

API reference improvements
It would be nice to systematically and extensively improve our API reference:

For every API endpoint, it would be nice to provide the following (example is the place where the property does not hold):

* Extensively document what the entity represents, whether it follows ISO-8601, **how the entity can be acquired**, and what time scale it comes from. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-day-of-week/) 
* Provide a minimum of how the entity can be used. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-fixed-offset-time-zone/)
* Thoroughly explain the allowed and expected contract and behaviours and system-specifics. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-clock/)
* If applicable, provide contexts in which the entity is expected/recommended to be used (e.g. our `README` shines at that) and how it interacts with other (core) entities. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-local-date-time/)
* Provide a basic usage example. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-time-zone/)
* If applicable, provide details about conversion rules, gotchas and recommendations. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-instant/)
* Where appropriate, information about conversions and interactions with native types (e.g. Instant <-> Java Instant conversion)
* Ensure that information used in our `README` is, in fact, deducible from the core entities documentation

## Additional Context from Issue Discussion

> If applicable, provide details about conversion rules, gotchas and recommendations. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-instant/)

I don't know of gotchas related to `Instant`. The only one that comes to mind is that `instant + 1.days` is a non-calendar operation (this could be worth documenting in the `plus` function), but I'd argue it's `Duration`'s gotcha. `Instant` seems tough to use incorrectly to me. What gotchas did you have in mind?

> Where appropriate, information about conversions and interactions with native types (e.g. Instant <-> Java Instant conversion)

When you have specific behavior on some platforms, you document it in the common code, makes sense; but when you have some extra interactions with platform code, you can't document them in common code (because symbols aren't resolved). If you have platform-specific documentation, in the IDE, it is either ignored (when writing common code) or replaces the common documentation. In the latter case, you have to repeat the common documentation for platforms *and* include platform-specific additions, which I think just isn't worth it.

If the system was a bit different and platform-specific documentation was *added* to the common documentation (and maybe the IDE would be smart enough to show the documentation for the platforms the project actually uses), then I agree that mentioning platform-specific converters and so on would be beneficial. Or better yet, if there was "extension documentation" along with extension functions: then we'd be able to show `NSDate` conversions in the `Instant` documentation for Darwin targets.

## Relevant Files

The following files are likely relevant to solving this issue:
- `README.md`
- `core/build.gradle.kts`
- `core/common/src/Clock.kt`
- `core/common/src/DateTimePeriod.kt`
- `core/common/src/DateTimeUnit.kt`
- `core/common/src/DayOfWeek.kt`
- `core/common/src/Exceptions.kt`
- `core/common/src/Instant.kt`
- `core/common/src/LocalDate.kt`
- `core/common/src/LocalDateTime.kt`
- `core/common/src/LocalTime.kt`
- `core/common/src/Month.kt`
- `core/common/src/TimeZone.kt`
- `core/common/src/UtcOffset.kt`
- `core/common/src/format/DateTimeComponents.kt`
- `core/common/src/format/DateTimeFormat.kt`
- `core/common/src/format/DateTimeFormatBuilder.kt`
- `core/common/src/format/LocalDateFormat.kt`
- `core/common/src/format/Unicode.kt`
- `core/common/src/serializers/DateTimePeriodSerializers.kt`
- `core/common/src/serializers/InstantSerializers.kt`
- `core/common/src/serializers/LocalDateSerializers.kt`
- `core/common/src/serializers/LocalDateTimeSerializers.kt`
- `core/common/src/serializers/LocalTimeSerializers.kt`
- `core/common/src/serializers/TimeZoneSerializers.kt`
- `core/commonJs/src/TimeZone.kt`
- `core/commonJs/src/UtcOffset.kt`
- `core/jvm/src/TimeZoneJvm.kt`
- `core/jvm/src/UtcOffsetJvm.kt`
- `core/native/src/TimeZone.kt`
- `core/native/src/UtcOffset.kt`

## Instructions

Please analyze this issue and implement a fix. Make the necessary code changes to resolve the problem described above.

Focus on:
- Understanding the root cause of the issue
- Making minimal, targeted changes to fix the problem
- Ensuring your changes don't break existing functionality

## Important Evaluation Notes

**Your work will be evaluated by running hidden tests that you do not have access to.** These tests will verify that your fix correctly addresses the issue. You should be confident in your solution before finishing.
**If you write tests to verify your changes, create them in NEW test files only** - do NOT modify existing test files

## CRITICAL RESTRICTIONS

**NEVER use git commands.** Do not run `git status`, `git diff`, `git checkout`, `git reset`, or ANY other git commands. Using git will corrupt the evaluation environment and invalidate your entire submission. All file operations should be done through normal file editing and reading tools, not git.

conversation_history.content:
1â†’# kotlinx-datetime
  2â†’
  3â†’[![Kotlin Alpha](https://kotl.in/badges/alpha.svg)](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/components-stability.html)
  4â†’[![JetBrains official project](https://jb.gg/badges/official.svg)](https://confluence.jetbrains.com/display/ALL/JetBrains+on+GitHub) 
  5â†’[![GitHub license](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-Apache%20License%202.0-blue.svg?style=flat)](http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
  6â†’[![Maven Central](https://img.shields.io/maven-central/v/org.jetbrains.kotlinx/kotlinx-datetime.svg?label=Maven%20Central)](https://search.maven.org/search?q=g:%22org.jetbrains.kotlinx%22%20AND%20a:%22kotlinx-datetime%22)
  7â†’[![Kotlin](https://img.shields.io/badge/kotlin-1.9.21-blue.svg?logo=kotlin)](http://kotlinlang.org)
  8â†’[![KDoc link](https://img.shields.io/badge/API_reference-KDoc-blue)](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/)
  9â†’[![Slack channel](https://img.shields.io/badge/chat-slack-blue.svg?logo=slack)](https://kotlinlang.slack.com/messages/kotlinx-datetime/)
 10â†’[![TeamCity build](https://img.shields.io/teamcity/build/s/KotlinTools_KotlinxDatetime_Build_All.svg?server=http%3A%2F%2Fteamcity.jetbrains.com)](https://teamcity.jetbrains.com/viewType.html?buildTypeId=KotlinTools_KotlinxDatetime_Build_All&guest=1)
 11â†’
 12â†’A multiplatform Kotlin library for working with date and time.
 13â†’
 14â†’See [Using in your projects](#using-in-your-projects) for the instructions how to setup a dependency in your project.
 15â†’
 16â†’## Design overview
 17â†’
 18â†’There are a few guiding principles in the design of `kotlinx-datetime`. First of all, it is pragmatic, focused
 19â†’on the most common problems developers face every day (pun intended) when working with dates and times. It is not
 20â†’all-encompassing and lacks some domain-specific utilities that special-purpose applications might need.
 21â†’We chose convenience over generality, so the API surface this library provides is as minimal as possible
 22â†’to meet the use-cases.
 23â†’
 24â†’The library puts a clear boundary between the physical time of an instant and the local, time-zone dependent 
 25â†’civil time, consisting of components such as year, month, etc that people use when talking about time. 
 26â†’We intentionally avoid entities in the library that mix both together and could be misused.
 27â†’However, there are convenience operations that take, for example, a physical instant and perform a calendar-based 
 28â†’adjustment (such as adding a month); all such operations
 29â†’explicitly take a time-zone information as parameter to clearly state that their result depends on the civil time-zone
 30â†’rules which are subject to change at any time.
 31â†’
 32â†’The library is based on the ISO 8601 international standard, other ways to represent dates and times are out of
 33â†’its scope. Internationalization (such as locale-specific month and day names) is out the scope, too. 
 34â†’
 35â†’## Types
 36â†’
 37â†’The library provides a basic set of types for working with date and time:
 38â†’
 39â†’- `Instant` to represent a moment on the UTC-SLS time scale;
 40â†’- `Clock` to obtain the current instant;
 41â†’- `LocalDateTime` to represent date and time components without a reference to the particular time zone; 
 42â†’- `LocalDate` to represent the components of date only;
 43â†’- `LocalTime` to represent the components of time only;
 44â†’- `TimeZone` and `FixedOffsetTimeZone` provide time zone information to convert between `Instant` and `LocalDateTime`;
 45â†’- `Month` and `DayOfWeek` enums;
 46â†’- `DateTimePeriod` to represent a difference between two instants decomposed into date and time units;
 47â†’- `DatePeriod` is a subclass of `DateTimePeriod` with zero time components,
 48â†’it represents a difference between two LocalDate values decomposed into date units.
 49â†’- `DateTimeUnit` provides a set of predefined date and time units to use in arithmetic operations on `Instant` and `LocalDate`. 
 50â†’- `UtcOffset` represents the amount of time the local date/time at a particular time zone differs from the date/time at UTC.
 51â†’
 52â†’### Type use-cases
 53â†’
 54â†’Here is some basic advice on how to choose which of the date-carrying types to use in what cases:
 55â†’
 56â†’- Use `Instant` to represent a timestamp of the event that had already happened in the past (like a timestamp of 
 57â†’  a log entry) or will definitely happen in a well-defined instant of time in the future not far away from now 
 58â†’  (like an order confirmation deadline in 1 hour from now).
 59â†’  
 60â†’- Use `LocalDateTime` to represent a time of the event that is scheduled to happen in the far future at a certain 
 61â†’  local time (like a scheduled meeting in a few months from now). You'll have to keep track of the `TimeZone` of 
 62â†’  the scheduled event separately. Try to avoid converting future events to `Instant` in advance, because time-zone 
 63â†’  rules might change unexpectedly in the future. In this [blog post](https://codeblog.jonskeet.uk/2019/03/27/storing-utc-is-not-a-silver-bullet/), you can read more about why it's not always 
 64â†’  a good idea to use `Instant` everywhere.
 65â†’  
 66â†’  Also, use `LocalDateTime` to decode an `Instant` to its local date-time components for display and UIs.
 67â†’  
 68â†’- Use `LocalDate` to represent the date of an event that does not have a specific time associated with it (like a birth date).
 69â†’
 70â†’- Use `LocalTime` to represent the time of an event that does not have a specific date associated with it.
 71â†’ 
 72â†’## Operations
 73â†’
 74â†’With the above types you can get the following operations done.
 75â†’
 76â†’
 77â†’### Getting the current moment of time
 78â†’
 79â†’The current moment of time can be captured with the `Instant` type. 
 80â†’To obtain an `Instant` corresponding to the current moment of time, 
 81â†’use `now()` function of the `Clock` interface:
 82â†’
 83â†’```kotlin
 84â†’val clock: Clock = ...
 85â†’val currentMoment = clock.now()
 86â†’```
 87â†’
 88â†’An instance of `Clock` can be injected through the function/class parameters, 
 89â†’or you can use its default implementation `Clock.System` that represents the system clock:
 90â†’
 91â†’```kotlin
 92â†’val currentMoment = Clock.System.now()
 93â†’```
 94â†’
 95â†’
 96â†’### Converting an instant to local date and time components
 97â†’
 98â†’An `Instant` is just a counter of high resolution time intervals since the beginning of time scale.
 99â†’To get human readable components from an `Instant` value, you need to convert it to the `LocalDateTime`
100â†’type that represents date and time components without a reference to the particular time zone.
101â†’
102â†’The `TimeZone` type provides the rules to convert instants from and to date/time components.
103â†’
104â†’```kotlin
105â†’val currentMoment: Instant = Clock.System.now()
106â†’val datetimeInUtc: LocalDateTime = currentMoment.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.UTC)
107â†’val datetimeInSystemZone: LocalDateTime = currentMoment.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
108â†’```
109â†’
110â†’A `LocalDateTime` instance exposes familiar components of the Gregorian calendar: 
111â†’`year`, `month`, `dayOfMonth`, `hour`, and so on up to `nanosecond`.
112â†’The property `dayOfWeek` shows what weekday that date is,
113â†’and `dayOfYear` shows the day number since the beginning of a year.
114â†’
115â†’
116â†’Additional time zones can be acquired by their string identifier with the `TimeZone.of(id: String)` function.
117â†’```kotlin
118â†’val tzBerlin = TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin")
119â†’val datetimeInBerlin = currentMoment.toLocalDateTime(tzBerlin)
120â†’```
121â†’
122â†’A `LocalDateTime` instance can be constructed from individual components:
123â†’
124â†’```kotlin
125â†’val kotlinReleaseDateTime = LocalDateTime(2016, 2, 15, 16, 57, 0, 0)
126â†’```
127â†’
128â†’An instant can be obtained from `LocalDateTime` by interpreting it as a time moment
129â†’in a particular `TimeZone`:
130â†’
131â†’```kotlin
132â†’val kotlinReleaseInstant = kotlinReleaseDateTime.toInstant(TimeZone.of("UTC+3"))
133â†’```
134â†’
135â†’### Getting local date components
136â†’
137â†’A `LocalDate` represents a local date without time. You can obtain one from an `Instant`
138â†’by converting it to `LocalDateTime` and taking its `date` property.
139â†’
140â†’```kotlin
141â†’val now: Instant = Clock.System.now()
142â†’val today: LocalDate = now.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault()).date
143â†’// or shorter
144â†’val today: LocalDate = Clock.System.todayIn(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
145â†’```
146â†’Note, that today's date really depends on the time zone in which you're observing the current moment.
147â†’
148â†’`LocalDate` can be constructed from three components, year, month, and day:
149â†’```kotlin
150â†’val knownDate = LocalDate(2020, 2, 21)
151â†’```
152â†’
153â†’### Getting local time components
154â†’
155â†’A `LocalTime` represents local time without date. You can obtain one from an `Instant`
156â†’by converting it to `LocalDateTime` and taking its `time` property.
157â†’
158â†’```kotlin
159â†’val now: Instant = Clock.System.now()
160â†’val thisTime: LocalTime = now.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault()).time
161â†’```
162â†’
163â†’A `LocalTime` can be constructed from four components, hour, minute, second and nanosecond:
164â†’```kotlin
165â†’val knownTime = LocalTime(hour = 23, minute = 59, second = 12)
166â†’val timeWithNanos = LocalTime(hour = 23, minute = 59, second = 12, nanosecond = 999)
167â†’val hourMinute = LocalTime(hour = 12, minute = 13)
168â†’```
169â†’
170â†’### Converting instant to and from unix time
171â†’
172â†’An `Instant` can be converted to a number of milliseconds since the Unix/POSIX epoch with the `toEpochMilliseconds()` function.
173â†’To convert back, use the companion object function `Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds(Long)`.
174â†’
175â†’### Converting instant and local date/time to and from the ISO 8601 string
176â†’
177â†’`Instant`, `LocalDateTime`, `LocalDate` and `LocalTime` provide shortcuts for
178â†’parsing and formatting them using the extended ISO-8601 format.
179â†’The `toString()` function is used to convert the value to a string in that format, and 
180â†’the `parse` function in companion object is used to parse a string representation back. 
181â†’
182â†’```kotlin
183â†’val instantNow = Clock.System.now()
184â†’instantNow.toString()  // returns something like 2015-12-31T12:30:00Z
185â†’val instantBefore = Instant.parse("2010-06-01T22:19:44.475Z")
186â†’```
187â†’
188â†’`LocalDateTime` uses a similar format, but without `Z` UTC time zone designator in the end.
189â†’
190â†’`LocalDate` uses a format with just year, month, and date components, e.g. `2010-06-01`.
191â†’
192â†’`LocalTime` uses a format with just hour, minute, second and (if non-zero) nanosecond components, e.g. `12:01:03`.
193â†’
194â†’```kotlin
195â†’LocalDateTime.parse("2010-06-01T22:19:44")
196â†’LocalDate.parse("2010-06-01")
197â†’LocalTime.parse("12:01:03")
198â†’LocalTime.parse("12:00:03.999")
199â†’LocalTime.parse("12:0:03.999") // fails with an IllegalArgumentException
200â†’```
201â†’
202â†’### Working with other string formats
203â†’
204â†’When some data needs to be formatted in some format other than ISO-8601, one
205â†’can define their own format or use some of the predefined ones:
206â†’
207â†’```kotlin
208â†’// import kotlinx.datetime.format.*
209â†’
210â†’val dateFormat = LocalDate.Format {
211â†’    monthNumber(padding = Padding.SPACE)
212â†’    char('/')
213â†’    dayOfMonth()
214â†’    char(' ')
215â†’    year()
216â†’}
217â†’
218â†’val date = dateFormat.parse("12/24 2023")
219â†’println(date.format(LocalDate.Formats.ISO_BASIC)) // "20231224"
220â†’```
221â†’
222â†’#### Using Unicode format strings (like `yyyy-MM-dd`)
223â†’
224â†’Given a constant format string like the ones used by Java's
225â†’[DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/api/java/time/format/DateTimeFormatter.html) can be
226â†’converted to Kotlin code using the following invocation:
227â†’
228â†’```kotlin
229â†’// import kotlinx.datetime.format.*
230â†’
231â†’println(DateTimeFormat.formatAsKotlinBuilderDsl(DateTimeComponents.Format {
232â†’    byUnicodePattern("uuuu-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss[.SSS]Z")
233â†’}))
234â†’
235â†’// will print:
236â†’/*
237â†’date(LocalDate.Formats.ISO)
238â†’char('T')
239â†’hour()
240â†’char(':')
241â†’minute()
242â†’char(':')
243â†’second()
244â†’alternativeParsing({
245â†’}) {
246â†’    char('.')
247â†’    secondFraction(3)
248â†’}
249â†’offset(UtcOffset.Formats.FOUR_DIGITS)
250â†’ */
251â†’```
252â†’
253â†’When your format string is not constant, with the `FormatStringsInDatetimeFormats` opt-in,
254â†’you can use the format without converting it to Kotlin code beforehand:
255â†’
256â†’```kotlin
257â†’val formatPattern = "yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss[.SSS]"
258â†’
259â†’@OptIn(FormatStringsInDatetimeFormats::class)
260â†’val dateTimeFormat = LocalDateTime.Format {
261â†’    byUnicodePattern(formatPattern)
262â†’}
263â†’
264â†’dateTimeFormat.parse("2023-12-24T23:59:59")
265â†’```
266â†’
267â†’### Parsing and formatting partial, compound or out-of-bounds data
268â†’
269â†’Sometimes, the required string format doesn't fully correspond to any of the
270â†’classes `kotlinx-datetime` provides. In these cases, `DateTimeComponents`, a
271â†’collection of all date-time fields, can be used instead.
272â†’
273â†’```kotlin
274â†’// import kotlinx.datetime.format.*
275â†’
276â†’val yearMonth = DateTimeComponents.Format { year(); char('-'); monthNumber() }
277â†’    .parse("2024-01")
278â†’println(yearMonth.year)
279â†’println(yearMonth.monthNumber)
280â†’
281â†’val dateTimeOffset = DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET
282â†’    .parse("2023-01-07T23:16:15.53+02:00")
283â†’println(dateTimeOffset.toUtcOffset()) // +02:00
284â†’println(dateTimeOffset.toLocalDateTime()) // 2023-01-07T23:16:15.53
285â†’```
286â†’
287â†’Occasionally, one can encounter strings where the values are slightly off:
288â†’for example, `23:59:60`, where `60` is an invalid value for the second.
289â†’`DateTimeComponents` allows parsing such values as well and then mutating them
290â†’before conversion.
291â†’
292â†’```kotlin
293â†’val time = DateTimeComponents.Format { time(LocalTime.Formats.ISO) }
294â†’    .parse("23:59:60").apply {
295â†’        if (second == 60) second = 59
296â†’    }.toLocalTime()
297â†’println(time) // 23:59:59
298â†’```
299â†’
300â†’Because `DateTimeComponents` is provided specifically for parsing and
301â†’formatting, there is no way to construct it normally. If one needs to format
302â†’partial, complex or out-of-bounds data, the `format` function allows building
303â†’`DateTimeComponents` specifically for formatting it:
304â†’
305â†’```kotlin
306â†’DateTimeComponents.Formats.RFC_1123.format {
307â†’    // the receiver of this lambda is DateTimeComponents
308â†’    setDate(LocalDate(2023, 1, 7))
309â†’    hour = 23
310â†’    minute = 59
311â†’    second = 60
312â†’    setOffset(UtcOffset(hours = 2))
313â†’} // Sat, 7 Jan 2023 23:59:60 +0200
314â†’```
315â†’
316â†’### Instant arithmetic
317â†’
318â†’```kotlin
319â†’val now = Clock.System.now()
320â†’val instantInThePast: Instant = Instant.parse("2020-01-01T00:00:00Z")
321â†’val durationSinceThen: Duration = now - instantInThePast
322â†’val equidistantInstantInTheFuture: Instant = now + durationSinceThen
323â†’```
324â†’
325â†’`Duration` is a type from the experimental `kotlin.time` package in the Kotlin standard library.
326â†’This type holds the amount of time that can be represented in different time units: from nanoseconds to 24H days.
327â†’
328â†’To get the calendar difference between two instants you can use the `Instant.periodUntil(Instant, TimeZone)` function.
329â†’
330â†’```kotlin
331â†’val period: DateTimePeriod = instantInThePast.periodUntil(Clock.System.now(), TimeZone.UTC)
332â†’```
333â†’
334â†’A `DateTimePeriod` represents a difference between two particular moments as a sum of calendar components, 
335â†’like "2 years, 3 months, 10 days, and 22 hours".
336â†’
337â†’The difference can be calculated as an integer amount of specified date or time units:
338â†’
339â†’```kotlin
340â†’val diffInMonths = instantInThePast.until(Clock.System.now(), DateTimeUnit.MONTH, TimeZone.UTC)
341â†’```
342â†’There are also shortcuts `yearsUntil(...)`, `monthsUntil(...)`, and `daysUntil(...)`.
343â†’
344â†’A particular amount of date/time units or a date/time period can be added to an `Instant` with the `plus` function:
345â†’
346â†’```kotlin
347â†’val now = Clock.System.now()
348â†’val systemTZ = TimeZone.currentSystemDefault()
349â†’val tomorrow = now.plus(2, DateTimeUnit.DAY, systemTZ)
350â†’val threeYearsAndAMonthLater = now.plus(DateTimePeriod(years = 3, months = 1), systemTZ)
351â†’```
352â†’
353â†’Note that `plus` and `...until` operations require a `TimeZone` as a parameter because the calendar interval between 
354â†’two particular instants can be different, when calculated in different time zones.
355â†’
356â†’### Date arithmetic
357â†’
358â†’Similar operations with date units are provided for `LocalDate` type:
359â†’
360â†’- `LocalDate.plus(number, DateTimeUnit.DateBased)`
361â†’- `LocalDate.plus(DatePeriod)`
362â†’- `LocalDate.until(LocalDate, DateTimeUnit.DateBased)` and the shortcuts `yearsUntil`, `monthUntil`, `daysUntil`
363â†’- `LocalDate.periodUntil(LocalDate): DatePeriod` and `LocalDate.minus(LocalDate): DatePeriod`
364â†’
365â†’Notice that, instead of the general `DateTimeUnit` and `DateTimePeriod` types, we're using their subtypes
366â†’`DateTimeUnit.DateBased` and `DatePeriod` respectively. This allows preventing the situations when
367â†’time components are being added to a date at compile time.
368â†’
369â†’### Date + time arithmetic
370â†’
371â†’Arithmetic on `LocalDateTime` is intentionally omitted. The reason for this is that the presence of daylight saving time
372â†’transitions (changing from standard time to daylight saving time and back) causes `LocalDateTime` arithmetic to be
373â†’ill-defined. For example, consider time gaps (or, as [`dst` tag wiki on Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/tags/dst/info)
374â†’calls them, "spring forward" transitions), that is, ranges of date + time combinations that never occur in a given
375â†’time zone due to clocks moving forward. If we allowed `LocalDateTime` arithmetic that ignored time zones, then it
376â†’could result in `LocalDateTime` instances that are inside a time gap and are invalid in the implied time zone.
377â†’
378â†’Therefore, the recommended way to use a `LocalDateTime` is to treat it as a representation of an `Instant`,
379â†’perform all the required arithmetic on `Instant` values, and only convert to `LocalDateTime` when a human-readable
380â†’representation is needed.
381â†’
382â†’```kotlin
383â†’val timeZone = TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin")
384â†’val localDateTime = LocalDateTime.parse("2021-03-27T02:16:20")
385â†’val instant = localDateTime.toInstant(timeZone)
386â†’
387â†’val instantOneDayLater = instant.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.DAY, timeZone)
388â†’val localDateTimeOneDayLater = instantOneDayLater.toLocalDateTime(timeZone)
389â†’// 2021-03-28T03:16:20, as 02:16:20 that day is in a time gap
390â†’
391â†’val instantTwoDaysLater = instant.plus(2, DateTimeUnit.DAY, timeZone)
392â†’val localDateTimeTwoDaysLater = instantTwoDaysLater.toLocalDateTime(timeZone)
393â†’// 2021-03-29T02:16:20
394â†’```
395â†’
396â†’## Implementation
397â†’
398â†’The implementation of date/time types, such as `Instant`, `LocalDateTime`, `TimeZone` and so on, relies on:
399â†’
400â†’- in JVM: [`java.time`](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/api/java/time/package-summary.html) API;
401â†’- in Js and Wasm-Js: [`js-joda`](https://js-joda.github.io/js-joda/) library;
402â†’- in Native: based on the [ThreeTen backport project](https://www.threeten.org/threetenbp/)
403â†’  - time zone support is provided by [date](https://github.com/HowardHinnant/date/) C++ library;
404â†’
405â†’## Known/open issues, work TBD
406â†’
407â†’- [x] Some kind of `Clock` interface is needed as a pluggable replacement for `Instant.now()`.
408â†’- [ ] Flexible locale-neutral parsing and formatting facilities are needed to support various date/time interchange
409â†’  formats that are used in practice (in particular, various RFCs).
410â†’
411â†’## Using in your projects
412â†’
413â†’> Note that the library is experimental, and the API is subject to change.
414â†’
415â†’The library is published to Maven Central.
416â†’
417â†’The library is compatible with the Kotlin Standard Library not lower than `1.9.0`.
418â†’
419â†’If you target Android devices running **below API 26**, you need to use Android Gradle plugin 4.0 or newer 
420â†’and enable [core library desugaring](https://developer.android.com/studio/write/java8-support#library-desugaring).
421â†’
422â†’### Gradle
423â†’
424â†’- Add the Maven Central repository if it is not already there:
425â†’
426â†’```kotlin
427â†’repositories {
428â†’    mavenCentral()
429â†’}
430â†’```
431â†’
432â†’- In multiplatform projects, add a dependency to the commonMain source set dependencies
433â†’```kotlin
434â†’kotlin {
435â†’    sourceSets {
436â†’        commonMain {
437â†’             dependencies {
438â†’                 implementation("org.jetbrains.kotlinx:kotlinx-datetime:0.6.0-RC.2")
439â†’             }
440â†’        }
441â†’    }
442â†’}
443â†’```
444â†’
445â†’- To use the library in a single-platform project, add a dependency to the dependencies block.
446â†’
447â†’```groovy
448â†’dependencies {
449â†’    implementation("org.jetbrains.kotlinx:kotlinx-datetime:0.6.0-RC.2")
450â†’}
451â†’```
452â†’
453â†’#### Note about time zones in JS
454â†’
455â†’By default, there's only one time zone available in Kotlin/JS: the `SYSTEM` time zone with a fixed offset.
456â†’
457â†’If you want to use all time zones in Kotlin/JS platform, you need to add the following npm dependency:
458â†’
459â†’```kotlin
460â†’kotlin {
461â†’    sourceSets {
462â†’        val jsMain by getting {
463â†’            dependencies {
464â†’                implementation(npm("@js-joda/timezone", "2.3.0"))
465â†’            }
466â†’        }
467â†’    }
468â†’}
469â†’```
470â†’
471â†’and after that add the following initialization code in your project:
472â†’
473â†’```kotlin
474â†’@JsModule("@js-joda/timezone")
475â†’@JsNonModule
476â†’external object JsJodaTimeZoneModule
477â†’
478â†’private val jsJodaTz = JsJodaTimeZoneModule
479â†’```
480â†’
481â†’### Maven
482â†’
483â†’Add a dependency to the `<dependencies>` element. Note that you need to use the platform-specific `-jvm` artifact in Maven.
484â†’
485â†’```xml
486â†’<dependency>
487â†’    <groupId>org.jetbrains.kotlinx</groupId>
488â†’    <artifactId>kotlinx-datetime-jvm</artifactId>
489â†’    <version>0.6.0-RC.2</version>
490â†’</dependency>
491â†’```
492â†’
493â†’## Building
494â†’
495â†’The project requires JDK 8 to build classes and to run tests. 
496â†’Gradle will try to find it among the installed JDKs or [provision](https://docs.gradle.org/current/userguide/toolchains.html#sec:provisioning) it automatically if it couldn't be found.
497â†’The path to JDK 8 can be additionally specified with the environment variable `JDK_8`. 
498â†’For local builds, you can use a later version of JDK if you don't have that 
499â†’version installed. Specify the version of this JDK with the `java.mainToolchainVersion` Gradle property.
500â†’
501â†’After that, the project can be opened in IDEA and built with Gradle.
502â†’
503â†’For building and running benchmarks, see [README.md](benchmarks/README.md)
504â†’

1â†’/*
  2â†’ * Copyright 2019-2020 JetBrains s.r.o.
  3â†’ * Use of this source code is governed by the Apache 2.0 License that can be found in the LICENSE.txt file.
  4â†’ */
  5â†’
  6â†’package kotlinx.datetime
  7â†’
  8â†’import kotlinx.datetime.format.*
  9â†’import kotlinx.datetime.internal.*
 10â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.InstantIso8601Serializer
 11â†’import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 12â†’import kotlin.time.*
 13â†’
 14â†’/**
 15â†’ * A moment in time.
 16â†’ *
 17â†’ * A point in time must be uniquely identified, so that it is independent of a time zone.
 18â†’ * For example, `1970-01-01, 00:00:00` does not represent a moment in time, since this would happen at different times
 19â†’ * in different time zones: someone in Tokyo would think its already `1970-01-01` several hours earlier than someone in
 20â†’ * Berlin would. To represent such entities, use [LocalDateTime].
 21â†’ * In contrast, "the moment the clocks in London first showed 00:00 on Jan 1, 2000" is a specific moment
 22â†’ * in time, as is "1970-01-01, 00:00:00 UTC+0", and so it can be represented as an [Instant].
 23â†’ *
 24â†’ * `Instant` uses the UTC-SLS (smeared leap second) time scale. This time scale doesn't contain instants
 25â†’ * corresponding to leap seconds, but instead "smears" positive and negative leap seconds among the last 1000 seconds
 26â†’ * of the day when a leap second happens.
 27â†’ *
 28â†’ * Some ways in which [Instant] can be acquired are:
 29â†’ * - [Clock.now] can be used to query the current moment for the given clock. With [Clock.System], it is the current
 30â†’ *   moment as the platform sees it.
 31â†’ * - [Instant.parse] parses an ISO-8601 string.
 32â†’ * - [Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds] and [Instant.fromEpochSeconds] construct the instant values from the amount of time
 33â†’ *   since `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` (the Unix epoch).
 34â†’ */
 35â†’@Serializable(with = InstantIso8601Serializer::class)
 36â†’public expect class Instant : Comparable<Instant> {
 37â†’
 38â†’    /**
 39â†’     * The number of seconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` rounded down to a [Long] number.
 40â†’     *
 41â†’     * The difference between the rounded number of seconds and the actual number of seconds
 42â†’     * is returned by [nanosecondsOfSecond] property expressed in nanoseconds.
 43â†’     *
 44â†’     * Note that this number doesn't include leap seconds added or removed since the epoch.
 45â†’     *
 46â†’     * @see Instant.fromEpochSeconds
 47â†’     */
 48â†’    public val epochSeconds: Long
 49â†’
 50â†’    /**
 51â†’     * The number of nanoseconds by which this instant is later than [epochSeconds] from the epoch instant.
 52â†’     *
 53â†’     * The value is always positive and lies in the range `0..999_999_999`.
 54â†’     *
 55â†’     * @see Instant.fromEpochSeconds
 56â†’     */
 57â†’    public val nanosecondsOfSecond: Int
 58â†’
 59â†’    /**
 60â†’     * Returns the number of milliseconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`.
 61â†’     *
 62â†’     * Any fractional part of millisecond is rounded down to the whole number of milliseconds.
 63â†’     *
 64â†’     * If the result does not fit in [Long], returns [Long.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Long.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
 65â†’     *
 66â†’     * @see Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds
 67â†’     */
 68â†’    public fun toEpochMilliseconds(): Long
 69â†’
 70â†’    /**
 71â†’     * Returns an instant that is the result of adding the specified [duration] to this instant.
 72â†’     *
 73â†’     * If the [duration] is positive, the returned instant is later than this instant.
 74â†’     * If the [duration] is negative, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
 75â†’     *
 76â†’     * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
 77â†’     */
 78â†’    public operator fun plus(duration: Duration): Instant
 79â†’
 80â†’    /**
 81â†’     * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting the specified [duration] from this instant.
 82â†’     *
 83â†’     * If the [duration] is positive, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
 84â†’     * If the [duration] is negative, the returned instant is later than this instant.
 85â†’     *
 86â†’     * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
 87â†’     */
 88â†’    public operator fun minus(duration: Duration): Instant
 89â†’
 90â†’    // questionable
 91â†’    /**
 92â†’     * Returns the [Duration] between two instants: [other] and `this`.
 93â†’     *
 94â†’     * The duration returned is positive if this instant is later than the other,
 95â†’     * and negative if this instant is earlier than the other.
 96â†’     *
 97â†’     * The result is never clamped, but note that for instants that are far apart,
 98â†’     * the value returned may represent the duration between them inexactly due to the loss of precision.
 99â†’     */
100â†’    public operator fun minus(other: Instant): Duration
101â†’
102â†’    /**
103â†’     * Compares `this` instant with the [other] instant.
104â†’     * Returns zero if this instant represents the same moment as the other (i.e. equal to other),
105â†’     * a negative number if this instant is earlier than the other,
106â†’     * and a positive number if this instant is later than the other.
107â†’     */
108â†’    public override operator fun compareTo(other: Instant): Int
109â†’
110â†’    /**
111â†’     * Converts this instant to the ISO-8601 string representation.
112â†’     *
113â†’     * The representation uses the UTC-SLS time scale, instead of UTC.
114â†’     * In practice, this means that leap second handling will not be readjusted to the UTC.
115â†’     * Leap seconds will not be added or skipped, so it is impossible to acquire a string
116â†’     * where the component for seconds is 60, and for any day, it's possible to observe 23:59:59.
117â†’     *
118â†’     * @see Instant.parse
119â†’     * @see DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET for a very similar format. The difference is that
120â†’     * [DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET] will not add trailing zeros for readability to the
121â†’     * fractional part of the second.
122â†’     */
123â†’    public override fun toString(): String
124â†’
125â†’
126â†’    public companion object {
127â†’        @Deprecated("Use Clock.System.now() instead", ReplaceWith("Clock.System.now()", "kotlinx.datetime.Clock"), level = DeprecationLevel.ERROR)
128â†’        public fun now(): Instant
129â†’
130â†’        /**
131â†’         * Returns an [Instant] that is [epochMilliseconds] number of milliseconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`.
132â†’         *
133â†’         * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
134â†’         *
135â†’         * @see Instant.toEpochMilliseconds
136â†’         */
137â†’        public fun fromEpochMilliseconds(epochMilliseconds: Long): Instant
138â†’
139â†’        /**
140â†’         * Returns an [Instant] that is the [epochSeconds] number of seconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`
141â†’         * and the [nanosecondAdjustment] number of nanoseconds from the whole second.
142â†’         *
143â†’         * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
144â†’         */
145â†’        public fun fromEpochSeconds(epochSeconds: Long, nanosecondAdjustment: Long = 0): Instant
146â†’
147â†’        /**
148â†’         * Returns an [Instant] that is the [epochSeconds] number of seconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`
149â†’         * and the [nanosecondAdjustment] number of nanoseconds from the whole second.
150â†’         *
151â†’         * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
152â†’         */
153â†’        public fun fromEpochSeconds(epochSeconds: Long, nanosecondAdjustment: Int): Instant
154â†’
155â†’        /**
156â†’         * A shortcut for calling [DateTimeFormat.parse], followed by [DateTimeComponents.toInstantUsingOffset].
157â†’         *
158â†’         * Parses a string that represents an instant including date and time components and a mandatory
159â†’         * time zone offset and returns the parsed [Instant] value.
160â†’         *
161â†’         * The string is considered to represent time on the UTC-SLS time scale instead of UTC.
162â†’         * In practice, this means that, even if there is a leap second on the given day, it will not affect how the
163â†’         * time is parsed, even if it's in the last 1000 seconds of the day.
164â†’         * Instead, even if there is a negative leap second on the given day, 23:59:59 is still considered valid time.
165â†’         * 23:59:60 is invalid on UTC-SLS, so parsing it will fail.
166â†’         *
167â†’         * If the format is not specified, [DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET] is used.
168â†’         *
169â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [Instant] are exceeded.
170â†’         *
171â†’         * @see Instant.toString for formatting using the default format.
172â†’         * @see Instant.format for formatting using a custom format.
173â†’         */
174â†’        public fun parse(
175â†’            input: CharSequence,
176â†’            format: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents> = DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET
177â†’        ): Instant
178â†’
179â†’        /**
180â†’         * An instant value that is far in the past.
181â†’         *
182â†’         * All instants in the range `DISTANT_PAST..DISTANT_FUTURE` can be [converted][Instant.toLocalDateTime] to
183â†’         * [LocalDateTime] without exceptions on all supported platforms.
184â†’         */
185â†’        public val DISTANT_PAST: Instant // -100001-12-31T23:59:59.999999999Z
186â†’
187â†’        /**
188â†’         * An instant value that is far in the future.
189â†’         *
190â†’         * All instants in the range `DISTANT_PAST..DISTANT_FUTURE` can be [converted][Instant.toLocalDateTime] to
191â†’         * [LocalDateTime] without exceptions on all supported platforms.
192â†’         */
193â†’        public val DISTANT_FUTURE: Instant // +100000-01-01T00:00:00Z
194â†’
195â†’        internal val MIN: Instant
196â†’        internal val MAX: Instant
197â†’    }
198â†’}
199â†’
200â†’/** Returns true if the instant is [Instant.DISTANT_PAST] or earlier. */
201â†’public val Instant.isDistantPast: Boolean
202â†’    get() = this <= Instant.DISTANT_PAST
203â†’
204â†’/** Returns true if the instant is [Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE] or later. */
205â†’public val Instant.isDistantFuture: Boolean
206â†’    get() = this >= Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE
207â†’
208â†’/**
209â†’ * @suppress
210â†’ */
211â†’@Deprecated("Removed to support more idiomatic code. See https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime/issues/339", ReplaceWith("Instant.parse(this)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
212â†’public fun String.toInstant(): Instant = Instant.parse(this)
213â†’
214â†’/**
215â†’ * Returns an instant that is the result of adding components of [DateTimePeriod] to this instant. The components are
216â†’ * added in the order from the largest units to the smallest, i.e. from years to nanoseconds.
217â†’ *
218â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the results of intermediate computations are too large to fit in
219â†’ * [LocalDateTime].
220â†’ */
221â†’public expect fun Instant.plus(period: DateTimePeriod, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant
222â†’
223â†’/**
224â†’ * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting components of [DateTimePeriod] from this instant. The components
225â†’ * are subtracted in the order from the largest units to the smallest, i.e. from years to nanoseconds.
226â†’ *
227â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the results of intermediate computations are too large to fit in
228â†’ * [LocalDateTime].
229â†’ */
230â†’public fun Instant.minus(period: DateTimePeriod, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant =
231â†’    /* An overflow can happen for any component, but we are only worried about nanoseconds, as having an overflow in
232â†’    any other component means that `plus` will throw due to the minimum value of the numeric type overflowing the
233â†’    platform-specific limits. */
234â†’    if (period.totalNanoseconds != Long.MIN_VALUE) {
235â†’        val negatedPeriod = with(period) { buildDateTimePeriod(-totalMonths, -days, -totalNanoseconds) }
236â†’        plus(negatedPeriod, timeZone)
237â†’    } else {
238â†’        val negatedPeriod = with(period) { buildDateTimePeriod(-totalMonths, -days, -(totalNanoseconds+1)) }
239â†’        plus(negatedPeriod, timeZone).plus(1, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND)
240â†’    }
241â†’
242â†’/**
243â†’ * Returns a [DateTimePeriod] representing the difference between `this` and [other] instants.
244â†’ *
245â†’ * The components of [DateTimePeriod] are calculated so that adding it to `this` instant results in the [other] instant.
246â†’ *
247â†’ * All components of the [DateTimePeriod] returned are:
248â†’ * - positive or zero if this instant is earlier than the other,
249â†’ * - negative or zero if this instant is later than the other,
250â†’ * - exactly zero if this instant is equal to the other.
251â†’ *
252â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if `this` or [other] instant is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
253â†’ *     Or (only on the JVM) if the number of months between the two dates exceeds an Int.
254â†’ */
255â†’public expect fun Instant.periodUntil(other: Instant, timeZone: TimeZone): DateTimePeriod
256â†’
257â†’/**
258â†’ * Returns the whole number of the specified date or time [units][unit] between `this` and [other] instants
259â†’ * in the specified [timeZone].
260â†’ *
261â†’ * The value returned is:
262â†’ * - positive or zero if this instant is earlier than the other,
263â†’ * - negative or zero if this instant is later than the other,
264â†’ * - zero if this instant is equal to the other.
265â†’ *
266â†’ * If the result does not fit in [Long], returns [Long.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Long.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
267â†’ *
268â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if `this` or [other] instant is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
269â†’ */
270â†’public expect fun Instant.until(other: Instant, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Long
271â†’
272â†’/**
273â†’ * Returns the whole number of the specified time [units][unit] between `this` and [other] instants.
274â†’ *
275â†’ * The value returned is:
276â†’ * - positive or zero if this instant is earlier than the other,
277â†’ * - negative or zero if this instant is later than the other,
278â†’ * - zero if this instant is equal to the other.
279â†’ *
280â†’ * If the result does not fit in [Long], returns [Long.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Long.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
281â†’ */
282â†’public fun Instant.until(other: Instant, unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Long =
283â†’    try {
284â†’        multiplyAddAndDivide(other.epochSeconds - epochSeconds,
285â†’            NANOS_PER_ONE.toLong(),
286â†’            (other.nanosecondsOfSecond - nanosecondsOfSecond).toLong(),
287â†’            unit.nanoseconds)
288â†’    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
289â†’        if (this < other) Long.MAX_VALUE else Long.MIN_VALUE
290â†’    }
291â†’
292â†’/**
293â†’ * Returns the number of whole days between two instants in the specified [timeZone].
294â†’ *
295â†’ * If the result does not fit in [Int], returns [Int.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Int.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
296â†’ *
297â†’ * @see Instant.until
298â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if `this` or [other] instant is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
299â†’ */
300â†’public fun Instant.daysUntil(other: Instant, timeZone: TimeZone): Int =
301â†’        until(other, DateTimeUnit.DAY, timeZone).clampToInt()
302â†’
303â†’/**
304â†’ * Returns the number of whole months between two instants in the specified [timeZone].
305â†’ *
306â†’ * If the result does not fit in [Int], returns [Int.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Int.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
307â†’ *
308â†’ * @see Instant.until
309â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if `this` or [other] instant is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
310â†’ */
311â†’public fun Instant.monthsUntil(other: Instant, timeZone: TimeZone): Int =
312â†’        until(other, DateTimeUnit.MONTH, timeZone).clampToInt()
313â†’
314â†’/**
315â†’ * Returns the number of whole years between two instants in the specified [timeZone].
316â†’ *
317â†’ * If the result does not fit in [Int], returns [Int.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Int.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
318â†’ *
319â†’ * @see Instant.until
320â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if `this` or [other] instant is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
321â†’ */
322â†’public fun Instant.yearsUntil(other: Instant, timeZone: TimeZone): Int =
323â†’        until(other, DateTimeUnit.YEAR, timeZone).clampToInt()
324â†’
325â†’/**
326â†’ * Returns a [DateTimePeriod] representing the difference between [other] and `this` instants.
327â†’ *
328â†’ * The components of [DateTimePeriod] are calculated so that adding it back to the `other` instant results in this instant.
329â†’ *
330â†’ * All components of the [DateTimePeriod] returned are:
331â†’ * - negative or zero if this instant is earlier than the other,
332â†’ * - positive or zero if this instant is later than the other,
333â†’ * - exactly zero if this instant is equal to the other.
334â†’ *
335â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if `this` or [other] instant is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
336â†’ *   Or (only on the JVM) if the number of months between the two dates exceeds an Int.
337â†’ * @see Instant.periodUntil
338â†’ */
339â†’public fun Instant.minus(other: Instant, timeZone: TimeZone): DateTimePeriod =
340â†’        other.periodUntil(this, timeZone)
341â†’
342â†’
343â†’/**
344â†’ * Returns an instant that is the result of adding one [unit] to this instant
345â†’ * in the specified [timeZone].
346â†’ *
347â†’ * The returned instant is later than this instant.
348â†’ *
349â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the result is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
350â†’ */
351â†’@Deprecated("Use the plus overload with an explicit number of units", ReplaceWith("this.plus(1, unit, timeZone)"))
352â†’public expect fun Instant.plus(unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant
353â†’
354â†’/**
355â†’ * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting one [unit] from this instant
356â†’ * in the specified [timeZone].
357â†’ *
358â†’ * The returned instant is earlier than this instant.
359â†’ *
360â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the result is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
361â†’ */
362â†’@Deprecated("Use the minus overload with an explicit number of units", ReplaceWith("this.minus(1, unit, timeZone)"))
363â†’public fun Instant.minus(unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant =
364â†’    plus(-1, unit, timeZone)
365â†’
366â†’/**
367â†’ * Returns an instant that is the result of adding one [unit] to this instant.
368â†’ *
369â†’ * The returned instant is later than this instant.
370â†’ *
371â†’ * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
372â†’ */
373â†’@Deprecated("Use the plus overload with an explicit number of units", ReplaceWith("this.plus(1, unit)"))
374â†’public fun Instant.plus(unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Instant =
375â†’    plus(1L, unit)
376â†’
377â†’/**
378â†’ * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting one [unit] from this instant.
379â†’ *
380â†’ * The returned instant is earlier than this instant.
381â†’ *
382â†’ * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
383â†’ */
384â†’@Deprecated("Use the minus overload with an explicit number of units", ReplaceWith("this.minus(1, unit)"))
385â†’public fun Instant.minus(unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Instant =
386â†’    plus(-1L, unit)
387â†’
388â†’/**
389â†’ * Returns an instant that is the result of adding the [value] number of the specified [unit] to this instant
390â†’ * in the specified [timeZone].
391â†’ *
392â†’ * If the [value] is positive, the returned instant is later than this instant.
393â†’ * If the [value] is negative, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
394â†’ *
395â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the result is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
396â†’ */
397â†’public expect fun Instant.plus(value: Int, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant
398â†’
399â†’/**
400â†’ * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting the [value] number of the specified [unit] from this instant
401â†’ * in the specified [timeZone].
402â†’ *
403â†’ * If the [value] is positive, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
404â†’ * If the [value] is negative, the returned instant is later than this instant.
405â†’ *
406â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the result is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
407â†’ */
408â†’public expect fun Instant.minus(value: Int, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant
409â†’
410â†’/**
411â†’ * Returns an instant that is the result of adding the [value] number of the specified [unit] to this instant.
412â†’ *
413â†’ * If the [value] is positive, the returned instant is later than this instant.
414â†’ * If the [value] is negative, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
415â†’ *
416â†’ * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
417â†’ */
418â†’public fun Instant.plus(value: Int, unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Instant =
419â†’    plus(value.toLong(), unit)
420â†’
421â†’/**
422â†’ * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting the [value] number of the specified [unit] from this instant.
423â†’ *
424â†’ * If the [value] is positive, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
425â†’ * If the [value] is negative, the returned instant is later than this instant.
426â†’ *
427â†’ * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
428â†’ */
429â†’public fun Instant.minus(value: Int, unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Instant =
430â†’    minus(value.toLong(), unit)
431â†’
432â†’/**
433â†’ * Returns an instant that is the result of adding the [value] number of the specified [unit] to this instant
434â†’ * in the specified [timeZone].
435â†’ *
436â†’ * If the [value] is positive, the returned instant is later than this instant.
437â†’ * If the [value] is negative, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
438â†’ *
439â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the result is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
440â†’ */
441â†’public expect fun Instant.plus(value: Long, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant
442â†’
443â†’/**
444â†’ * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting the [value] number of the specified [unit] from this instant
445â†’ * in the specified [timeZone].
446â†’ *
447â†’ * If the [value] is positive, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
448â†’ * If the [value] is negative, the returned instant is later than this instant.
449â†’ *
450â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the result is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
451â†’ */
452â†’public fun Instant.minus(value: Long, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant =
453â†’    if (value != Long.MIN_VALUE) {
454â†’        plus(-value, unit, timeZone)
455â†’    } else {
456â†’        plus(-(value + 1), unit, timeZone).plus(1, unit, timeZone)
457â†’    }
458â†’
459â†’/**
460â†’ * Returns an instant that is the result of adding the [value] number of the specified [unit] to this instant.
461â†’ *
462â†’ * If the [value] is positive, the returned instant is later than this instant.
463â†’ * If the [value] is negative, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
464â†’ *
465â†’ * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
466â†’ */
467â†’public expect fun Instant.plus(value: Long, unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Instant
468â†’
469â†’/**
470â†’ * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting the [value] number of the specified [unit] from this instant.
471â†’ *
472â†’ * If the [value] is positive, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
473â†’ * If the [value] is negative, the returned instant is later than this instant.
474â†’ *
475â†’ * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
476â†’ */
477â†’public fun Instant.minus(value: Long, unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Instant =
478â†’    if (value != Long.MIN_VALUE) {
479â†’        plus(-value, unit)
480â†’    } else {
481â†’        plus(-(value + 1), unit).plus(1, unit)
482â†’    }
483â†’
484â†’/**
485â†’ * Returns the whole number of the specified date or time [units][unit] between [other] and `this` instants
486â†’ * in the specified [timeZone].
487â†’ *
488â†’ * The value returned is negative or zero if this instant is earlier than the other,
489â†’ * and positive or zero if this instant is later than the other.
490â†’ *
491â†’ * If the result does not fit in [Long], returns [Long.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Long.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
492â†’ *
493â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if `this` or [other] instant is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
494â†’ * @see Instant.until
495â†’ */
496â†’public fun Instant.minus(other: Instant, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Long =
497â†’        other.until(this, unit, timeZone)
498â†’
499â†’/**
500â†’ * Returns the whole number of the specified time [units][unit] between [other] and `this` instants.
501â†’ *
502â†’ * The value returned is negative or zero if this instant is earlier than the other,
503â†’ * and positive or zero if this instant is later than the other.
504â†’ *
505â†’ * If the result does not fit in [Long], returns [Long.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Long.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
506â†’ *
507â†’ * @see Instant.until
508â†’ */
509â†’public fun Instant.minus(other: Instant, unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Long =
510â†’    other.until(this, unit)
511â†’
512â†’/**
513â†’ * Formats this value using the given [format] using the given [offset].
514â†’ *
515â†’ * Equivalent to calling [DateTimeFormat.format] on [format] and using [DateTimeComponents.setDateTimeOffset] in
516â†’ * the lambda.
517â†’ *
518â†’ * [DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET] is a format very similar to the one used by [toString].
519â†’ * The only difference is that [Instant.toString] adds trailing zeros to the fraction-of-second component so that the
520â†’ * number of digits after a dot is a multiple of three.
521â†’ */
522â†’public fun Instant.format(format: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents>, offset: UtcOffset = UtcOffset.ZERO): String {
523â†’    val instant = this
524â†’    return format.format { setDateTimeOffset(instant, offset) }
525â†’}
526â†’
527â†’internal const val DISTANT_PAST_SECONDS = -3217862419201
528â†’internal const val DISTANT_FUTURE_SECONDS = 3093527980800
529â†’

1â†’/*
  2â†’ * Copyright 2019-2022 JetBrains s.r.o. and contributors.

  4â†’ */
  5â†’
  6â†’package kotlinx.datetime
  7â†’
  8â†’import kotlinx.datetime.format.*
  9â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.LocalDateIso8601Serializer
 10â†’import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 11â†’
 12â†’/**
 13â†’ * The date part of [LocalDateTime].
 14â†’ *
 15â†’ * This class represents dates without a reference to a particular time zone.
 16â†’ * As such, these objects may denote different spans of time in different time zones: for someone in Berlin,
 17â†’ * `2020-08-30` started and ended at different moments from those for someone in Tokyo.
 18â†’ *
 19â†’ * The arithmetic on [LocalDate] values is defined independently of the time zone (so `2020-08-30` plus one day
 20â†’ * is `2020-08-31` everywhere): see various [LocalDate.plus] and [LocalDate.minus] functions, as well
 21â†’ * as [LocalDate.periodUntil] and various other [*until][LocalDate.daysUntil] functions.
 22â†’ */
 23â†’@Serializable(with = LocalDateIso8601Serializer::class)
 24â†’public expect class LocalDate : Comparable<LocalDate> {
 25â†’    public companion object {
 26â†’        /**
 27â†’         * A shortcut for calling [DateTimeFormat.parse].
 28â†’         *
 29â†’         * Parses a string that represents a date and returns the parsed [LocalDate] value.
 30â†’         *
 31â†’         * If [format] is not specified, [Formats.ISO] is used.
 32â†’         *
 33â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [LocalDate] are exceeded.
 34â†’         *
 35â†’         * @see LocalDate.toString for formatting using the default format.
 36â†’         * @see LocalDate.format for formatting using a custom format.
 37â†’         */
 38â†’        public fun parse(input: CharSequence, format: DateTimeFormat<LocalDate> = getIsoDateFormat()): LocalDate
 39â†’
 40â†’        /**
 41â†’         * Returns a [LocalDate] that is [epochDays] number of days from the epoch day `1970-01-01`.
 42â†’         *
 43â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the result exceeds the platform-specific boundaries of [LocalDate].
 44â†’         *
 45â†’         * @see LocalDate.toEpochDays
 46â†’         */
 47â†’        public fun fromEpochDays(epochDays: Int): LocalDate
 48â†’
 49â†’        /**
 50â†’         * Creates a new format for parsing and formatting [LocalDate] values.
 51â†’         *
 52â†’         * Example:
 53â†’         * ```
 54â†’         * // 2020 Jan 05
 55â†’         * LocalDate.Format {
 56â†’         *   year()
 57â†’         *   char(' ')
 58â†’         *   monthName(MonthNames.ENGLISH_ABBREVIATED)
 59â†’         *   char(' ')
 60â†’         *   dayOfMonth()
 61â†’         * }
 62â†’         * ```
 63â†’         *
 64â†’         * Only parsing and formatting of well-formed values is supported. If the input does not fit the boundaries
 65â†’         * (for example, [dayOfMonth] is 31 for February), consider using [DateTimeComponents.Format] instead.
 66â†’         *
 67â†’         * There is a collection of predefined formats in [LocalDate.Formats].
 68â†’         *
 69â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if parsing using this format is ambiguous.
 70â†’         */
 71â†’        @Suppress("FunctionName")
 72â†’        public fun Format(block: DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDate.() -> Unit): DateTimeFormat<LocalDate>
 73â†’
 74â†’        internal val MIN: LocalDate
 75â†’        internal val MAX: LocalDate
 76â†’    }
 77â†’
 78â†’    /**
 79â†’     * A collection of predefined formats for parsing and formatting [LocalDate] values.
 80â†’     *
 81â†’     * See [LocalDate.Formats.ISO] and [LocalDate.Formats.ISO_BASIC] for popular predefined formats.
 82â†’     * [LocalDate.parse] and [LocalDate.toString] can be used as convenient shortcuts for the
 83â†’     * [LocalDate.Formats.ISO] format.
 84â†’     *
 85â†’     * If predefined formats are not sufficient, use [LocalDate.Format] to create a custom
 86â†’     * [kotlinx.datetime.format.DateTimeFormat] for [LocalDate] values.
 87â†’     */
 88â†’    public object Formats {
 89â†’        /**
 90â†’         * ISO 8601 extended format, which is the format used by [LocalDate.toString] and [LocalDate.parse].
 91â†’         *
 92â†’         * Examples of dates in ISO 8601 format:
 93â†’         * - `2020-08-30`
 94â†’         * - `+12020-08-30`
 95â†’         * - `0000-08-30`
 96â†’         * - `-0001-08-30`
 97â†’         */
 98â†’        public val ISO: DateTimeFormat<LocalDate>
 99â†’
100â†’        /**
101â†’         * ISO 8601 basic format.
102â†’         *
103â†’         * Examples of dates in ISO 8601 basic format:
104â†’         * - `20200830`
105â†’         * - `+120200830`
106â†’         * - `00000830`
107â†’         * - `-00010830`
108â†’         */
109â†’        public val ISO_BASIC: DateTimeFormat<LocalDate>
110â†’    }
111â†’
112â†’    /**
113â†’     * Constructs a [LocalDate] instance from the given date components.
114â†’     *
115â†’     * The components [monthNumber] and [dayOfMonth] are 1-based.
116â†’     *
117â†’     * The supported ranges of components:
118â†’     * - [year] the range is platform dependent, but at least is enough to represent dates of all instants between
119â†’     *          [Instant.DISTANT_PAST] and [Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE]
120â†’     * - [monthNumber] `1..12`
121â†’     * - [dayOfMonth] `1..31`, the upper bound can be less, depending on the month
122â†’     *
123â†’     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if any parameter is out of range, or if [dayOfMonth] is invalid for the
124â†’     * given [monthNumber] and [year].
125â†’     */
126â†’    public constructor(year: Int, monthNumber: Int, dayOfMonth: Int)
127â†’
128â†’    /**
129â†’     * Constructs a [LocalDate] instance from the given date components.
130â†’     *
131â†’     * The supported ranges of components:
132â†’     * - [year] the range is platform dependent, but at least is enough to represent dates of all instants between
133â†’     *          [Instant.DISTANT_PAST] and [Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE]
134â†’     * - [month] all values of the [Month] enum
135â†’     * - [dayOfMonth] `1..31`, the upper bound can be less, depending on the month
136â†’     *
137â†’     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if any parameter is out of range, or if [dayOfMonth] is invalid for the
138â†’     * given [month] and [year].
139â†’     */
140â†’    public constructor(year: Int, month: Month, dayOfMonth: Int)
141â†’
142â†’    /** Returns the year component of the date. */
143â†’    public val year: Int
144â†’
145â†’    /** Returns the number-of-month (1..12) component of the date. */
146â†’    public val monthNumber: Int
147â†’
148â†’    /** Returns the month ([Month]) component of the date. */
149â†’    public val month: Month
150â†’
151â†’    /** Returns the day-of-month component of the date. */
152â†’    public val dayOfMonth: Int
153â†’
154â†’    /** Returns the day-of-week component of the date. */
155â†’    public val dayOfWeek: DayOfWeek
156â†’
157â†’    /** Returns the day-of-year component of the date. */
158â†’    public val dayOfYear: Int
159â†’
160â†’    /**
161â†’     * Returns the number of days since the epoch day `1970-01-01`.
162â†’     *
163â†’     * If the result does not fit in [Int], returns [Int.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Int.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
164â†’     *
165â†’     * @see LocalDate.fromEpochDays
166â†’     */
167â†’    public fun toEpochDays(): Int
168â†’
169â†’    /**
170â†’     * Compares `this` date with the [other] date.
171â†’     * Returns zero if this date represent the same day as the other (i.e. equal to other),
172â†’     * a negative number if this date is earlier than the other,
173â†’     * and a positive number if this date is later than the other.
174â†’     */
175â†’    public override fun compareTo(other: LocalDate): Int
176â†’
177â†’    /**
178â†’     * Converts this date to the extended ISO-8601 string representation.
179â†’     *
180â†’     * @see Formats.ISO for the format details.
181â†’     * @see parse for the dual operation: obtaining [LocalDate] from a string.
182â†’     * @see LocalDate.format for formatting using a custom format.
183â†’     */
184â†’    public override fun toString(): String
185â†’}
186â†’
187â†’/**
188â†’ * Formats this value using the given [format].
189â†’ * Equivalent to calling [DateTimeFormat.format] on [format] with `this`.
190â†’ */
191â†’public fun LocalDate.format(format: DateTimeFormat<LocalDate>): String = format.format(this)
192â†’
193â†’/**
194â†’ * @suppress
195â†’ */
196â†’@Deprecated("Removed to support more idiomatic code. See https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime/issues/339", ReplaceWith("LocalDate.parse(this)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
197â†’public fun String.toLocalDate(): LocalDate = LocalDate.parse(this)
198â†’
199â†’/**
200â†’ * Combines this date's components with the specified time components into a [LocalDateTime] value.
201â†’ *
202â†’ * For finding an instant that corresponds to the start of a date in a particular time zone consider using
203â†’ * [LocalDate.atStartOfDayIn] function because a day does not always start at the fixed time 0:00:00.
204â†’ */
205â†’public fun LocalDate.atTime(hour: Int, minute: Int, second: Int = 0, nanosecond: Int = 0): LocalDateTime =
206â†’    LocalDateTime(year, monthNumber, dayOfMonth, hour, minute, second, nanosecond)
207â†’
208â†’/**
209â†’ * Combines this date's components with the specified [LocalTime] components into a [LocalDateTime] value.
210â†’ *
211â†’ * For finding an instant that corresponds to the start of a date in a particular time zone consider using
212â†’ * [LocalDate.atStartOfDayIn] function because a day does not always start at the fixed time 0:00:00.
213â†’ */
214â†’public fun LocalDate.atTime(time: LocalTime): LocalDateTime = LocalDateTime(this, time)
215â†’
216â†’
217â†’/**
218â†’ * Returns a date that is the result of adding components of [DatePeriod] to this date. The components are
219â†’ * added in the order from the largest units to the smallest, i.e. from years to days.
220â†’ *
221â†’ * @see LocalDate.periodUntil
222â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the results of intermediate computations are too large to fit in
223â†’ * [LocalDate].
224â†’ */
225â†’public expect operator fun LocalDate.plus(period: DatePeriod): LocalDate
226â†’
227â†’/**
228â†’ * Returns a date that is the result of subtracting components of [DatePeriod] from this date. The components are
229â†’ * subtracted in the order from the largest units to the smallest, i.e. from years to days.
230â†’ *
231â†’ * @see LocalDate.periodUntil
232â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the results of intermediate computations are too large to fit in
233â†’ * [LocalDate].
234â†’ */
235â†’public operator fun LocalDate.minus(period: DatePeriod): LocalDate =
236â†’    if (period.days != Int.MIN_VALUE && period.months != Int.MIN_VALUE) {
237â†’        plus(with(period) { DatePeriod(-years, -months, -days) })
238â†’    } else {
239â†’        minus(period.years, DateTimeUnit.YEAR).minus(period.months, DateTimeUnit.MONTH)
240â†’            .minus(period.days, DateTimeUnit.DAY)
241â†’    }
242â†’
243â†’/**
244â†’ * Returns a [DatePeriod] representing the difference between `this` and [other] dates.
245â†’ *
246â†’ * The components of [DatePeriod] are calculated so that adding it to `this` date results in the [other] date.
247â†’ *
248â†’ * All components of the [DatePeriod] returned are:
249â†’ * - positive or zero if this date is earlier than the other,
250â†’ * - negative or zero if this date is later than the other,
251â†’ * - exactly zero if this date is equal to the other.
252â†’ *
253â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if the number of months between the two dates exceeds an Int (JVM only).
254â†’ *
255â†’ * @see LocalDate.minus
256â†’ */
257â†’public expect fun LocalDate.periodUntil(other: LocalDate): DatePeriod
258â†’
259â†’/**
260â†’ * Returns a [DatePeriod] representing the difference between [other] and `this` dates.
261â†’ *
262â†’ * The components of [DatePeriod] are calculated so that adding it back to the `other` date results in this date.
263â†’ *
264â†’ * All components of the [DatePeriod] returned are:
265â†’ * - negative or zero if this date is earlier than the other,
266â†’ * - positive or zero if this date is later than the other,
267â†’ * - exactly zero if this date is equal to the other.
268â†’ *
269â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if the number of months between the two dates exceeds an Int (JVM only).
270â†’ *
271â†’ * @see LocalDate.periodUntil
272â†’ */
273â†’public operator fun LocalDate.minus(other: LocalDate): DatePeriod = other.periodUntil(this)
274â†’
275â†’/**
276â†’ * Returns the whole number of the specified date [units][unit] between `this` and [other] dates.
277â†’ *
278â†’ * The value returned is:
279â†’ * - positive or zero if this date is earlier than the other,
280â†’ * - negative or zero if this date is later than the other,
281â†’ * - zero if this date is equal to the other.
282â†’
283â†’ * If the result does not fit in [Int], returns [Int.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Int.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
284â†’ *
285â†’ * @see LocalDate.daysUntil
286â†’ * @see LocalDate.monthsUntil
287â†’ * @see LocalDate.yearsUntil
288â†’ *
289â†’ */
290â†’public expect fun LocalDate.until(other: LocalDate, unit: DateTimeUnit.DateBased): Int
291â†’
292â†’/**
293â†’ * Returns the number of whole days between two dates.
294â†’ *
295â†’ * If the result does not fit in [Int], returns [Int.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Int.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
296â†’ *
297â†’ * @see LocalDate.until
298â†’ */
299â†’public expect fun LocalDate.daysUntil(other: LocalDate): Int
300â†’
301â†’/**
302â†’ * Returns the number of whole months between two dates.
303â†’ *
304â†’ * If the result does not fit in [Int], returns [Int.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Int.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
305â†’ *
306â†’ * @see LocalDate.until
307â†’ */
308â†’public expect fun LocalDate.monthsUntil(other: LocalDate): Int
309â†’
310â†’/**
311â†’ * Returns the number of whole years between two dates.
312â†’ *
313â†’ * If the result does not fit in [Int], returns [Int.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Int.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
314â†’ *
315â†’ * @see LocalDate.until
316â†’ */
317â†’public expect fun LocalDate.yearsUntil(other: LocalDate): Int
318â†’
319â†’/**
320â†’ * Returns a [LocalDate] that is the result of adding one [unit] to this date.
321â†’ *
322â†’ * The returned date is later than this date.
323â†’ *
324â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if the result exceeds the boundaries of [LocalDate].
325â†’ */
326â†’@Deprecated("Use the plus overload with an explicit number of units", ReplaceWith("this.plus(1, unit)"))
327â†’public expect fun LocalDate.plus(unit: DateTimeUnit.DateBased): LocalDate
328â†’
329â†’/**
330â†’ * Returns a [LocalDate] that is the result of subtracting one [unit] from this date.
331â†’ *
332â†’ * The returned date is earlier than this date.
333â†’ *
334â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if the result exceeds the boundaries of [LocalDate].
335â†’ */
336â†’@Deprecated("Use the minus overload with an explicit number of units", ReplaceWith("this.minus(1, unit)"))
337â†’public fun LocalDate.minus(unit: DateTimeUnit.DateBased): LocalDate = plus(-1, unit)
338â†’
339â†’/**
340â†’ * Returns a [LocalDate] that is the result of adding the [value] number of the specified [unit] to this date.
341â†’ *
342â†’ * If the [value] is positive, the returned date is later than this date.
343â†’ * If the [value] is negative, the returned date is earlier than this date.
344â†’ *
345â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if the result exceeds the boundaries of [LocalDate].
346â†’ */
347â†’public expect fun LocalDate.plus(value: Int, unit: DateTimeUnit.DateBased): LocalDate
348â†’
349â†’/**
350â†’ * Returns a [LocalDate] that is the result of subtracting the [value] number of the specified [unit] from this date.
351â†’ *
352â†’ * If the [value] is positive, the returned date is earlier than this date.
353â†’ * If the [value] is negative, the returned date is later than this date.
354â†’ *
355â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if the result exceeds the boundaries of [LocalDate].
356â†’ */
357â†’public expect fun LocalDate.minus(value: Int, unit: DateTimeUnit.DateBased): LocalDate
358â†’
359â†’/**
360â†’ * Returns a [LocalDate] that is the result of adding the [value] number of the specified [unit] to this date.
361â†’ *
362â†’ * If the [value] is positive, the returned date is later than this date.
363â†’ * If the [value] is negative, the returned date is earlier than this date.
364â†’ *
365â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if the result exceeds the boundaries of [LocalDate].
366â†’ */
367â†’public expect fun LocalDate.plus(value: Long, unit: DateTimeUnit.DateBased): LocalDate
368â†’
369â†’/**
370â†’ * Returns a [LocalDate] that is the result of subtracting the [value] number of the specified [unit] from this date.
371â†’ *
372â†’ * If the [value] is positive, the returned date is earlier than this date.
373â†’ * If the [value] is negative, the returned date is later than this date.
374â†’ *
375â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if the result exceeds the boundaries of [LocalDate].
376â†’ */
377â†’public fun LocalDate.minus(value: Long, unit: DateTimeUnit.DateBased): LocalDate = plus(-value, unit)
378â†’
379â†’// workaround for https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/KT-65484
380â†’internal fun getIsoDateFormat() = LocalDate.Formats.ISO
381â†’
