  4â†’ */
  5â†’
  6â†’package kotlinx.datetime
  7â†’
  8â†’import kotlinx.datetime.internal.*
  9â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.DatePeriodIso8601Serializer
 10â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer
 11â†’import kotlin.math.*
 12â†’import kotlin.time.Duration
 13â†’import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 14â†’
 15â†’/**
 16â†’ * A difference between two [instants][Instant], decomposed into date and time components.
 17â†’ *
 18â†’ * The date components are: [years], [months], [days].
 19â†’ *
 20â†’ * The time components are: [hours], [minutes], [seconds], [nanoseconds].
 21â†’ *
 22â†’ * A `DateTimePeriod` can be constructed using the same-named constructor function,
 23â†’ * [parsed][DateTimePeriod.parse] from a string, or returned as the result of instant arithmetic operations (see [Instant.periodUntil]).
 24â†’ * All these functions can return a [DatePeriod] value, which is a subtype of `DateTimePeriod`,
 25â†’ * a special case that only stores date components, if all time components of the result happen to be zero.
 26â†’ */
 27â†’@Serializable(with = DateTimePeriodIso8601Serializer::class)
 28â†’// TODO: could be error-prone without explicitly named params
 29â†’public sealed class DateTimePeriod {
 30â†’    internal abstract val totalMonths: Int
 31â†’
 32â†’    /**
 33â†’     * The number of calendar days.
 34â†’     *
 35â†’     * Note that a calendar day is not identical to 24 hours, see [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for details.
 36â†’     */
 37â†’    public abstract val days: Int
 38â†’    internal abstract val totalNanoseconds: Long
 39â†’
 40â†’    /**
 41â†’     * The number of whole years.
 42â†’     */
 43â†’    public val years: Int get() = totalMonths / 12
 44â†’
 45â†’    /**
 46â†’     * The number of months in this period that don't form a whole year, so this value is always in `(-11..11)`.
 47â†’     */
 48â†’    public val months: Int get() = totalMonths % 12
 49â†’
 50â†’    /**
 51â†’     * The number of whole hours in this period.
 52â†’     */
 53â†’    public open val hours: Int get() = (totalNanoseconds / 3_600_000_000_000).toInt()
 54â†’
 55â†’    /**
 56â†’     * The number of whole minutes in this period that don't form a whole hour, so this value is always in `(-59..59)`.
 57â†’     */
 58â†’    public open val minutes: Int get() = ((totalNanoseconds % 3_600_000_000_000) / 60_000_000_000).toInt()
 59â†’
 60â†’    /**
 61â†’     * The number of whole seconds in this period that don't form a whole minute, so this value is always in `(-59..59)`.
 62â†’     */
 63â†’    public open val seconds: Int get() = ((totalNanoseconds % 60_000_000_000) / NANOS_PER_ONE).toInt()
 64â†’
 65â†’    /**
 66â†’     * The number of whole nanoseconds in this period that don't form a whole second, so this value is always in
 67â†’     * `(-999_999_999..999_999_999)`.
 68â†’     */
 69â†’    public open val nanoseconds: Int get() = (totalNanoseconds % NANOS_PER_ONE).toInt()
 70â†’
 71â†’    private fun allNonpositive() =
 72â†’        totalMonths <= 0 && days <= 0 && totalNanoseconds <= 0 && (totalMonths or days != 0 || totalNanoseconds != 0L)
 73â†’
 74â†’    /**
 75â†’     * Converts this period to the ISO-8601 string representation for durations.
 76â†’     *
 77â†’     * @see DateTimePeriod.parse
 78â†’     */
 79â†’    override fun toString(): String = buildString {
 80â†’        val sign = if (allNonpositive()) { append('-'); -1 } else 1
 81â†’        append('P')
 82â†’        if (years != 0) append(years * sign).append('Y')
 83â†’        if (months != 0) append(months * sign).append('M')
 84â†’        if (days != 0) append(days * sign).append('D')
 85â†’        var t = "T"
 86â†’        if (hours != 0) append(t).append(hours * sign).append('H').also { t = "" }
 87â†’        if (minutes != 0) append(t).append(minutes * sign).append('M').also { t = "" }
 88â†’        if (seconds or nanoseconds != 0) {
 89â†’            append(t)
 90â†’            append(when {
 91â†’                seconds != 0 -> seconds * sign
 92â†’                nanoseconds * sign < 0 -> "-0"
 93â†’                else -> "0"
 94â†’            })
 95â†’            if (nanoseconds != 0) append('.').append((nanoseconds.absoluteValue).toString().padStart(9, '0'))
 96â†’            append('S')
 97â†’        }
 98â†’
 99â†’        if (length == 1) append("0D")
100â†’    }
101â†’
102â†’    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean {
103â†’        if (this === other) return true
104â†’        if (other !is DateTimePeriod) return false
105â†’
106â†’        if (totalMonths != other.totalMonths) return false
107â†’        if (days != other.days) return false
108â†’        if (totalNanoseconds != other.totalNanoseconds) return false
109â†’
110â†’        return true
111â†’    }
112â†’
113â†’    override fun hashCode(): Int {
114â†’        var result = totalMonths
115â†’        result = 31 * result + days
116â†’        result = 31 * result + totalNanoseconds.hashCode()
117â†’        return result
118â†’    }
119â†’
120â†’    public companion object {
121â†’        /**
122â†’         * Parses a ISO-8601 duration string as a [DateTimePeriod].
123â†’         * If the time components are absent or equal to zero, returns a [DatePeriod].
124â†’         *
125â†’         * Additionally, we support the `W` signifier to represent weeks.
126â†’         *
127â†’         * Examples of durations in the ISO-8601 format:
128â†’         * - `P1Y40D` is one year and 40 days
129â†’         * - `-P1DT1H` is minus (one day and one hour)
130â†’         * - `P1DT-1H` is one day minus one hour
131â†’         * - `-PT0.000000001S` is minus one nanosecond
132â†’         *
133â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [DateTimePeriod] are
134â†’         * exceeded.
135â†’         */
136â†’        public fun parse(text: String): DateTimePeriod {
137â†’            fun parseException(message: String, position: Int): Nothing =
138â†’                throw DateTimeFormatException("Parse error at char $position: $message")
139â†’            val START = 0
140â†’            val AFTER_P = 1
141â†’            val AFTER_YEAR = 2
142â†’            val AFTER_MONTH = 3
143â†’            val AFTER_WEEK = 4
144â†’            val AFTER_DAY = 5
145â†’            val AFTER_T = 6
146â†’            val AFTER_HOUR = 7
147â†’            val AFTER_MINUTE = 8
148â†’            val AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO = 9
149â†’
150â†’            var state = START
151â†’            // next unread character
152â†’            var i = 0
153â†’            var sign = 1
154â†’            var years = 0
155â†’            var months = 0
156â†’            var weeks = 0
157â†’            var days = 0
158â†’            var hours = 0
159â†’            var minutes = 0
160â†’            var seconds = 0
161â†’            var nanoseconds = 0
162â†’            while (true) {
163â†’                if (i >= text.length) {
164â†’                    if (state == START)
165â†’                        parseException("Unexpected end of input; 'P' designator is required", i)
166â†’                    if (state == AFTER_T)
167â†’                        parseException("Unexpected end of input; at least one time component is required after 'T'", i)
168â†’                    val daysTotal = when (val n = days.toLong() + weeks * 7) {
169â†’                        in Int.MIN_VALUE..Int.MAX_VALUE -> n.toInt()
170â†’                        else -> parseException("The total number of days under 'D' and 'W' designators should fit into an Int", 0)
171â†’                    }
172â†’                    return DateTimePeriod(years, months, daysTotal, hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds.toLong())
173â†’                }
174â†’                if (state == START) {
175â†’                    if (i + 1 >= text.length && (text[i] == '+' || text[i] == '-'))
176â†’                        parseException("Unexpected end of string; 'P' designator is required", i)
177â†’                    when (text[i]) {
178â†’                        '+', '-' -> {
179â†’                            if (text[i] == '-')
180â†’                                sign = -1
181â†’                            if (text[i + 1] != 'P')
182â†’                                parseException("Expected 'P', got '${text[i + 1]}'", i + 1)
183â†’                            i += 2
184â†’                        }
185â†’                        'P' -> { i += 1 }
186â†’                        else -> parseException("Expected '+', '-', 'P', got '${text[i]}'", i)
187â†’                    }
188â†’                    state = AFTER_P
189â†’                    continue
190â†’                }
191â†’                var localSign = sign
192â†’                val iStart = i
193â†’                when (text[i]) {
194â†’                    '+', '-' -> {
195â†’                        if (text[i] == '-') localSign *= -1
196â†’                        i += 1
197â†’                        if (i >= text.length || text[i] !in '0'..'9')
198â†’                            parseException("A number expected after '${text[i]}'", i)
199â†’                    }
200â†’                    in '0'..'9' -> { }
201â†’                    'T' -> {
202â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_T)
203â†’                            parseException("Only one 'T' designator is allowed", i)
204â†’                        state = AFTER_T
205â†’                        i += 1
206â†’                        continue
207â†’                    }
208â†’                }
209â†’                var number = 0L
210â†’                while (i < text.length && text[i] in '0'..'9') {
211â†’                    try {
212â†’                        number = safeAdd(safeMultiply(number, 10), (text[i] - '0').toLong())
213â†’                    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
214â†’                        parseException("The number is too large", iStart)
215â†’                    }
216â†’                    i += 1
217â†’                }
218â†’                number *= localSign
219â†’                if (i == text.length)
220â†’                    parseException("Expected a designator after the numerical value", i)
221â†’                val wrongOrder = "Wrong component order: should be 'Y', 'M', 'W', 'D', then designator 'T', then 'H', 'M', 'S'"
222â†’                fun Long.toIntThrowing(component: Char): Int {
223â†’                    if (this < Int.MIN_VALUE || this > Int.MAX_VALUE)
224â†’                        parseException("Value $this does not fit into an Int, which is required for component '$component'", iStart)
225â†’                    return toInt()
226â†’                }
227â†’                when (text[i].uppercaseChar()) {
228â†’                    'Y' -> {
229â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_YEAR)
230â†’                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
231â†’                        state = AFTER_YEAR
232â†’                        years = number.toIntThrowing('Y')
233â†’                    }
234â†’                    'M' -> {
235â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_T) {
236â†’                            // Minutes
237â†’                            if (state >= AFTER_MINUTE)
238â†’                                parseException(wrongOrder, i)
239â†’                            state = AFTER_MINUTE
240â†’                            minutes = number.toIntThrowing('M')
241â†’                        } else {
242â†’                            // Months
243â†’                            if (state >= AFTER_MONTH)
244â†’                                parseException(wrongOrder, i)
245â†’                            state = AFTER_MONTH
246â†’                            months = number.toIntThrowing('M')
247â†’                        }
248â†’                    }
249â†’                    'W' -> {
250â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_WEEK)
251â†’                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
252â†’                        state = AFTER_WEEK
253â†’                        weeks = number.toIntThrowing('W')
254â†’                    }
255â†’                    'D' -> {
256â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_DAY)
257â†’                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
258â†’                        state = AFTER_DAY
259â†’                        days = number.toIntThrowing('D')
260â†’                    }
261â†’                    'H' -> {
262â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_HOUR || state < AFTER_T)
263â†’                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
264â†’                        state = AFTER_HOUR
265â†’                        hours = number.toIntThrowing('H')
266â†’                    }
267â†’                    'S' -> {
268â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO || state < AFTER_T)
269â†’                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
270â†’                        state = AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO
271â†’                        seconds = number.toIntThrowing('S')
272â†’                    }
273â†’                    '.', ',' -> {
274â†’                        i += 1
275â†’                        if (i >= text.length)
276â†’                            parseException("Expected designator 'S' after ${text[i - 1]}", i)
277â†’                        val iStartFraction = i
278â†’                        while (i < text.length && text[i] in '0'..'9')
279â†’                            i += 1
280â†’                        val fractionLength = i - iStartFraction
281â†’                        if (fractionLength > 9)
282â†’                            parseException("Only the nanosecond fractions of a second are supported", iStartFraction)
283â†’                        val fractionalPart = text.substring(iStartFraction, i) + "0".repeat(9 - fractionLength)
284â†’                        nanoseconds = fractionalPart.toInt(10) * localSign
285â†’                        if (text[i] != 'S')
286â†’                            parseException("Expected the 'S' designator after a fraction", i)
287â†’                        if (state >= AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO || state < AFTER_T)
288â†’                            parseException(wrongOrder, i)
289â†’                        state = AFTER_SECOND_AND_NANO
290â†’                        seconds = number.toIntThrowing('S')
291â†’                    }
292â†’                    else -> parseException("Expected a designator after the numerical value", i)
293â†’                }
294â†’                i += 1
295â†’           }
296â†’        }
297â†’    }
298â†’}
299â†’
300â†’/**
301â†’ * @suppress
302â†’ */
303â†’@Deprecated("Removed to support more idiomatic code. See https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime/issues/339", ReplaceWith("DateTimePeriod.parse(this)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
304â†’public fun String.toDateTimePeriod(): DateTimePeriod = DateTimePeriod.parse(this)
305â†’
306â†’/**
307â†’ * A special case of [DateTimePeriod] that only stores date components and has all time components equal to zero.
308â†’ *
309â†’ * A `DatePeriod` is automatically returned from all constructor functions for [DateTimePeriod] if it turns out that
310â†’ * the time components are zero.
311â†’ *
312â†’ * `DatePeriod` values are used in operations on [LocalDates][LocalDate] and are returned from operations on [LocalDates][LocalDate],
313â†’ * but they also can be passed anywhere a [DateTimePeriod] is expected.
314â†’ */
315â†’@Serializable(with = DatePeriodIso8601Serializer::class)
316â†’public class DatePeriod internal constructor(
317â†’    internal override val totalMonths: Int,
318â†’    override val days: Int,
319â†’) : DateTimePeriod() {
320â†’    public constructor(years: Int = 0, months: Int = 0, days: Int = 0): this(totalMonths(years, months), days)
321â†’    // avoiding excessive computations
322â†’    /** The number of whole hours in this period. Always equal to zero. */
323â†’    override val hours: Int get() = 0
324â†’
325â†’    /** The number of whole minutes in this period. Always equal to zero. */
326â†’    override val minutes: Int get() = 0
327â†’
328â†’    /** The number of whole seconds in this period. Always equal to zero. */
329â†’    override val seconds: Int get() = 0
330â†’
331â†’    /** The number of nanoseconds in this period. Always equal to zero. */
332â†’    override val nanoseconds: Int get() = 0
333â†’    internal override val totalNanoseconds: Long get() = 0
334â†’
335â†’    public companion object {
336â†’        /**
337â†’         * Parses the ISO-8601 duration representation as a [DatePeriod].
338â†’         *
339â†’         * This function is equivalent to [DateTimePeriod.parse], but will fail if any of the time components are not
340â†’         * zero.
341â†’         *
342â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed, the boundaries of [DatePeriod] are exceeded,
343â†’         * or any time components are not zero.
344â†’         *
345â†’         * @see DateTimePeriod.parse
346â†’         */
347â†’        public fun parse(text: String): DatePeriod =
348â†’            when (val period = DateTimePeriod.parse(text)) {
349â†’                is DatePeriod -> period
350â†’                else -> throw DateTimeFormatException("Period $period (parsed from string $text) is not date-based")
351â†’            }
352â†’    }
353â†’}
354â†’
355â†’/**
356â†’ * @suppress
357â†’ */
358â†’@Deprecated("Removed to support more idiomatic code. See https://github.com/Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime/issues/339", ReplaceWith("DatePeriod.parse(this)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
359â†’public fun String.toDatePeriod(): DatePeriod = DatePeriod.parse(this)
360â†’
361â†’private class DateTimePeriodImpl(
362â†’    internal override val totalMonths: Int,
363â†’    override val days: Int,
364â†’    internal override val totalNanoseconds: Long,
365â†’) : DateTimePeriod()
366â†’
367â†’// TODO: these calculations fit in a JS Number. Possible to do an expect/actual here.
368â†’private fun totalMonths(years: Int, months: Int): Int =
369â†’    when (val totalMonths = years.toLong() * 12 + months.toLong()) {
370â†’        in Int.MIN_VALUE..Int.MAX_VALUE -> totalMonths.toInt()
371â†’        else -> throw IllegalArgumentException("The total number of months in $years years and $months months overflows an Int")
372â†’    }
373â†’
374â†’private fun totalNanoseconds(hours: Int, minutes: Int, seconds: Int, nanoseconds: Long): Long {
375â†’    val totalMinutes: Long = hours.toLong() * 60 + minutes
376â†’    // absolute value at most 61 * Int.MAX_VALUE
377â†’    val totalMinutesAsSeconds: Long = totalMinutes * 60
378â†’    // absolute value at most 61 * 60 * Int.MAX_VALUE < 64 * 64 * 2^31 = 2^43
379â†’    val minutesAndNanosecondsAsSeconds: Long = totalMinutesAsSeconds + nanoseconds / NANOS_PER_ONE
380â†’    // absolute value at most 2^43 + 2^63 / 10^9 < 2^43 + 2^34 < 2^44
381â†’    val totalSeconds = minutesAndNanosecondsAsSeconds + seconds
382â†’    // absolute value at most 2^44 + 2^31 < 2^45
383â†’    return try {
384â†’        multiplyAndAdd(totalSeconds, 1_000_000_000, nanoseconds % NANOS_PER_ONE)
385â†’    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
386â†’        throw IllegalArgumentException("The total number of nanoseconds in $hours hours, $minutes minutes, $seconds seconds, and $nanoseconds nanoseconds overflows a Long")
387â†’    }
388â†’}
389â†’
390â†’internal fun buildDateTimePeriod(totalMonths: Int = 0, days: Int = 0, totalNanoseconds: Long): DateTimePeriod =
391â†’    if (totalNanoseconds != 0L)
392â†’        DateTimePeriodImpl(totalMonths, days, totalNanoseconds)
393â†’    else
394â†’        DatePeriod(totalMonths, days)
395â†’
396â†’/**
397â†’ * Constructs a new [DateTimePeriod]. If all the time components are zero, returns a [DatePeriod].
398â†’ *
399â†’ * It is recommended to always explicitly name the arguments when constructing this manually,
400â†’ * like `DateTimePeriod(years = 1, months = 12)`.
401â†’ *
402â†’ * The passed numbers are not stored as is but are normalized instead for human readability, so, for example,
403â†’ * `DateTimePeriod(months = 24)` becomes `DateTimePeriod(years = 2)`.
404â†’ *
405â†’ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the total number of months in [years] and [months] overflows an [Int].
406â†’ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the total number of months in [hours], [minutes], [seconds] and [nanoseconds]
407â†’ * overflows a [Long].
408â†’ */
409â†’public fun DateTimePeriod(
410â†’    years: Int = 0,
411â†’    months: Int = 0,
412â†’    days: Int = 0,
413â†’    hours: Int = 0,
414â†’    minutes: Int = 0,
415â†’    seconds: Int = 0,
416â†’    nanoseconds: Long = 0
417â†’): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(totalMonths(years, months), days,
418â†’    totalNanoseconds(hours, minutes, seconds, nanoseconds))
419â†’
420â†’/**
421â†’ * Constructs a [DateTimePeriod] from a [Duration].
422â†’ *
423â†’ * If the duration value is too big to be represented as a [Long] number of nanoseconds,
424â†’ * the result will be [Long.MAX_VALUE] nanoseconds.
425â†’ */
426â†’// TODO: maybe it's more consistent to throw here on overflow?
427â†’public fun Duration.toDateTimePeriod(): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(totalNanoseconds = inWholeNanoseconds)
428â†’
429â†’/**
430â†’ * Adds two [DateTimePeriod] instances.
431â†’ *
432â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if arithmetic overflow happens.
433â†’ */
434â†’public operator fun DateTimePeriod.plus(other: DateTimePeriod): DateTimePeriod = buildDateTimePeriod(
435â†’    safeAdd(totalMonths, other.totalMonths),
436â†’    safeAdd(days, other.days),
437â†’    safeAdd(totalNanoseconds, other.totalNanoseconds),
438â†’)
439â†’
440â†’/**
441â†’ * Adds two [DatePeriod] instances.
442â†’ *
443â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if arithmetic overflow happens.
444â†’ */
445â†’public operator fun DatePeriod.plus(other: DatePeriod): DatePeriod = DatePeriod(
446â†’    safeAdd(totalMonths, other.totalMonths),
447â†’    safeAdd(days, other.days),
448â†’)
449â†’

{"file_path": "/project/core/common/src/Clock.kt", "old_string": "/**
 * A source of [Instant] values.
 *
 * See [Clock.System][Clock.System] for the clock instance that queries the operating system.
 */
public interface Clock {
    /**
     * Returns the [Instant] corresponding to the current time, according to this clock.
     */
    public fun now(): Instant

    /**
     * The [Clock] instance that queries the operating system as its source of knowledge of time.
     */
    public object System : Clock {
        override fun now(): Instant = @Suppress("DEPRECATION_ERROR") Instant.now()
    }", "new_string": "/**
 * A source of [Instant] values.
 *
 * The primary purpose of [Clock] is to provide access to the current moment in time.
 * The most common implementation is [Clock.System], which queries the operating system's clock.
 *
 * ## Acquiring the current time
 *
 * To obtain the current moment in time as an [Instant]:
 * ```
 * val now = Clock.System.now()
 * ```
 *
 * To obtain the current date in a specific time zone:
 * ```
 * val today = Clock.System.todayIn(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
 * ```
 *
 * ## Using Clock for testability
 *
 * The [Clock] interface is designed to be injectable, making it easy to test time-dependent code.
 * Instead of directly calling [Clock.System], you can pass a [Clock] instance as a parameter:
 *
 * ```
 * class EventLogger(private val clock: Clock) {
 *     fun logEvent(message: String) {
 *         val timestamp = clock.now()
 *         println("[$timestamp] $message")
 *     }
 * }
 * ```
 *
 * In production code, pass [Clock.System], and in tests, provide a test double that returns
 * predetermined [Instant] values.
 *
 * ## System clock behavior
 *
 * [Clock.System] reflects the system clock, which means:
 * - The returned [Instant] represents time on the UTC-SLS time scale (smeared leap seconds).
 * - The precision depends on the platform but is at least millisecond-level.
 * - The clock can jump backward or forward if the system time is adjusted (e.g., by NTP synchronization
 *   or manual user changes).
 * - On some platforms, the clock may not be monotonic. For measuring elapsed time or timeouts,
 *   consider using [Clock.asTimeSource] which provides a [TimeSource] suitable for such purposes.
 *
 * @see Instant for the representation of moments in time
 * @see TimeZone for converting instants to local date-time values
 */
public interface Clock {
    /**
     * Returns the [Instant] corresponding to the current time, according to this clock.
     *
     * Example:
     * ```
     * val currentMoment = clock.now()
     * println("Current time: $currentMoment")
     * ```
     */
    public fun now(): Instant

    /**
     * The [Clock] instance that queries the operating system as its source of knowledge of time.
     *
     * This is the primary way to obtain the current time in production code.
     *
     * Example:
     * ```
     * val now = Clock.System.now()
     * val currentDate = Clock.System.todayIn(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
     * ```
     *
     * @see now
     * @see todayIn
     */
    public object System : Clock {
        override fun now(): Instant = @Suppress("DEPRECATION_ERROR") Instant.now()
    }"}

 * Returns the current date at the given [time zone][timeZone], according to [this Clock][this].
 *
 * The date is determined by converting the current [Instant] from this clock to a [LocalDateTime]
 * in the specified [timeZone] and extracting its date component.
 *
 * Note that the current date depends on the time zone. For example, at the same moment,
 * it might be March 15 in New York but already March 16 in Tokyo.
 *
 * Example:
 * ```
 * val todayInBerlin = Clock.System.todayIn(TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
 * val todayInTokyo = Clock.System.todayIn(TimeZone.of("Asia/Tokyo"))
 * val todayHere = Clock.System.todayIn(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
 * ```
 *
 * @see Instant.toLocalDateTime
 * @see LocalDateTime.date
 */

{"file_path": "/project/core/common/src/DayOfWeek.kt", "old_string": "/**
 * The enumeration class representing the days of the week.
 */
public expect enum class DayOfWeek {
    MONDAY,
    TUESDAY,
    WEDNESDAY,
    THURSDAY,
    FRIDAY,
    SATURDAY,
    SUNDAY;
}", "new_string": "/**
 * The enumeration class representing the days of the week.
 *
 * This enum follows the ISO-8601 standard, where Monday is the first day of the week (day 1)
 * and Sunday is the last day (day 7).
 *
 * ## Acquiring a DayOfWeek
 *
 * A [DayOfWeek] value can be obtained in several ways:
 * - From a [LocalDate] via its [LocalDate.dayOfWeek] property
 * - From a [LocalDateTime] via its [LocalDateTime.dayOfWeek] property
 * - By constructing from an ISO day number using [DayOfWeek] function: `DayOfWeek(1)` returns [MONDAY]
 * - Directly using the enum constants: [MONDAY], [TUESDAY], etc.
 *
 * ## ISO-8601 compliance
 *
 * The [isoDayNumber] property returns the ISO-8601 day-of-week number:
 * - [MONDAY] = 1
 * - [TUESDAY] = 2
 * - [WEDNESDAY] = 3
 * - [THURSDAY] = 4
 * - [FRIDAY] = 5
 * - [SATURDAY] = 6
 * - [SUNDAY] = 7
 *
 * ## Usage examples
 *
 * Checking if a date falls on a weekend:
 * ```
 * val date = LocalDate(2024, 1, 15)
 * val isWeekend = date.dayOfWeek in setOf(DayOfWeek.SATURDAY, DayOfWeek.SUNDAY)
 * ```
 *
 * Getting the day of week from an instant:
 * ```
 * val instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val dayOfWeek = instant.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault()).dayOfWeek
 * println("Today is $dayOfWeek")
 * ```
 *
 * Constructing from ISO day number:
 * ```
 * val monday = DayOfWeek(1)  // MONDAY
 * val sunday = DayOfWeek(7)  // SUNDAY
 * ```
 *
 * @see LocalDate.dayOfWeek
 * @see LocalDateTime.dayOfWeek
 * @see isoDayNumber
 */
public expect enum class DayOfWeek {
    /** Monday, the first day of the week according to ISO-8601. */
    MONDAY,
    
    /** Tuesday, the second day of the week according to ISO-8601. */
    TUESDAY,
    
    /** Wednesday, the third day of the week according to ISO-8601. */
    WEDNESDAY,
    
    /** Thursday, the fourth day of the week according to ISO-8601. */
    THURSDAY,
    
    /** Friday, the fifth day of the week according to ISO-8601. */
    FRIDAY,
    
    /** Saturday, the sixth day of the week according to ISO-8601. */
    SATURDAY,
    
    /** Sunday, the seventh day of the week according to ISO-8601. */
    SUNDAY;
}"}

{"file_path": "/project/core/common/src/Instant.kt", "old_string": "/**
 * A moment in time.
 *
 * A point in time must be uniquely identified, so that it is independent of a time zone.
 * For example, `1970-01-01, 00:00:00` does not represent a moment in time, since this would happen at different times
 * in different time zones: someone in Tokyo would think its already `1970-01-01` several hours earlier than someone in
 * Berlin would. To represent such entities, use [LocalDateTime].
 * In contrast, "the moment the clocks in London first showed 00:00 on Jan 1, 2000" is a specific moment
 * in time, as is "1970-01-01, 00:00:00 UTC+0", and so it can be represented as an [Instant].
 *
 * `Instant` uses the UTC-SLS (smeared leap second) time scale. This time scale doesn't contain instants
 * corresponding to leap seconds, but instead "smears" positive and negative leap seconds among the last 1000 seconds
 * of the day when a leap second happens.
 *
 * Some ways in which [Instant] can be acquired are:
 * - [Clock.now] can be used to query the current moment for the given clock. With [Clock.System], it is the current
 *   moment as the platform sees it.
 * - [Instant.parse] parses an ISO-8601 string.
 * - [Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds] and [Instant.fromEpochSeconds] construct the instant values from the amount of time
 *   since `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` (the Unix epoch).
 */", "new_string": "/**
 * A moment in time.
 *
 * A point in time must be uniquely identified, so that it is independent of a time zone.
 * For example, `1970-01-01, 00:00:00` does not represent a moment in time, since this would happen at different times
 * in different time zones: someone in Tokyo would think its already `1970-01-01` several hours earlier than someone in
 * Berlin would. To represent such entities, use [LocalDateTime].
 * In contrast, "the moment the clocks in London first showed 00:00 on Jan 1, 2000" is a specific moment
 * in time, as is "1970-01-01, 00:00:00 UTC+0", and so it can be represented as an [Instant].
 *
 * ## Time scale
 *
 * [Instant] uses the UTC-SLS (smeared leap second) time scale. This time scale doesn't contain instants
 * corresponding to leap seconds, but instead "smears" positive and negative leap seconds among the last 1000 seconds
 * of the day when a leap second happens. This means:
 * - You will never see a second value of 60
 * - Every day has exactly 86400 seconds
 * - During a leap second event, time progresses slightly slower or faster than usual
 *
 * ## Acquiring an Instant
 *
 * An [Instant] can be obtained in several ways:
 *
 * **From the current time:**
 * ```
 * val now = Clock.System.now()
 * ```
 *
 * **From epoch milliseconds or seconds:**
 * ```
 * val instant1 = Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds(1609459200000) // 2021-01-01T00:00:00Z
 * val instant2 = Instant.fromEpochSeconds(1609459200, 500_000_000) // with nanoseconds
 * ```
 *
 * **By parsing an ISO-8601 string:**
 * ```
 * val instant = Instant.parse("2021-01-01T00:00:00Z")
 * ```
 *
 * **From LocalDateTime and a TimeZone:**
 * ```
 * val localDateTime = LocalDateTime(2021, 1, 1, 0, 0)
 * val instant = localDateTime.toInstant(TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
 * ```
 *
 * ## Usage
 *
 * [Instant] is ideal for representing:
 * - Timestamps of events that already happened (e.g., log entries, database records)
 * - Near-future events with a well-defined moment (e.g., "order must be confirmed within 1 hour")
 *
 * For far-future scheduled events (like meetings), consider using [LocalDateTime] with a [TimeZone]
 * stored separately, as time zone rules may change.
 *
 * ## Operations
 *
 * **Arithmetic with Duration (physical time):**
 * ```
 * val later = instant + 5.hours
 * val earlier = instant - 30.minutes
 * val duration = instant2 - instant1
 * ```
 *
 * **Arithmetic with calendar units (requires TimeZone):**
 * ```
 * val nextDay = instant.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.DAY, TimeZone.UTC)
 * val nextMonth = instant.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.MONTH, timeZone)
 * val daysBetween = instant1.daysUntil(instant2, timeZone)
 * ```
 *
 * Note: Adding `1.days` as a [Duration] adds exactly 24 hours, which may differ from adding 1 calendar day
 * due to daylight saving time transitions.
 *
 * **Converting to human-readable components:**
 * ```
 * val localDateTime = instant.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
 * println("Year: ${localDateTime.year}, Month: ${localDateTime.month}")
 * ```
 *
 * **Formatting and parsing:**
 * ```
 * val str = instant.toString() // ISO-8601 format, e.g., "2021-01-01T00:00:00Z"
 * val parsed = Instant.parse(str)
 * ```
 *
 * ## Boundaries
 *
 * [Instant] has platform-specific boundaries, but [DISTANT_PAST] and [DISTANT_FUTURE] are guaranteed
 * to be representable on all platforms and can be safely converted to [LocalDateTime].
 *
 * @see Clock for obtaining the current instant
 * @see LocalDateTime for date/time without a time zone
 * @see TimeZone for converting between Instant and LocalDateTime
 * @see DateTimeUnit for calendar-based arithmetic
 */"}

{"file_path": "/project/core/common/src/LocalDate.kt", "old_string": "/**
 * The date part of [LocalDateTime].
 *
 * This class represents dates without a reference to a particular time zone.
 * As such, these objects may denote different spans of time in different time zones: for someone in Berlin,
 * `2020-08-30` started and ended at different moments from those for someone in Tokyo.
 *
 * The arithmetic on [LocalDate] values is defined independently of the time zone (so `2020-08-30` plus one day
 * is `2020-08-31` everywhere): see various [LocalDate.plus] and [LocalDate.minus] functions, as well
 * as [LocalDate.periodUntil] and various other [*until][LocalDate.daysUntil] functions.
 */", "new_string": "/**
 * The date part of [LocalDateTime].
 *
 * This class represents dates without a reference to a particular time zone.
 * As such, these objects may denote different spans of time in different time zones: for someone in Berlin,
 * `2020-08-30` started and ended at different moments from those for someone in Tokyo.
 *
 * ## ISO-8601 compliance
 *
 * [LocalDate] follows the ISO-8601 calendar system, which is based on the proleptic Gregorian calendar.
 * This means the Gregorian calendar rules are extended backwards indefinitely, so dates before the
 * introduction of the Gregorian calendar (1582) are also represented using these rules.
 *
 * ## Acquiring a LocalDate
 *
 * A [LocalDate] can be obtained in several ways:
 *
 * **From the current date in a time zone:**
 * ```
 * val today = Clock.System.todayIn(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
 * ```
 *
 * **By constructing from components:**
 * ```
 * val date1 = LocalDate(2023, 4, 15)  // April 15, 2023
 * val date2 = LocalDate(2023, Month.APRIL, 15)  // using Month enum
 * ```
 *
 * **By parsing an ISO-8601 string:**
 * ```
 * val date = LocalDate.parse("2023-04-15")
 * ```
 *
 * **From an Instant and TimeZone:**
 * ```
 * val instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val date = instant.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.UTC).date
 * ```
 *
 * **From epoch days:**
 * ```
 * val date = LocalDate.fromEpochDays(19000) // days since 1970-01-01
 * ```
 *
 * ## Usage
 *
 * [LocalDate] is ideal for representing:
 * - Dates without specific times (e.g., birth dates, holidays, deadlines)
 * - Calendar dates that don't depend on time zones
 * - Date ranges and periods
 *
 * **When to use LocalDate vs Instant:**
 * - Use [LocalDate] for dates that are meaningful in a calendar context (birthdays, holidays)
 * - Use [Instant] for timestamps of specific moments (event logs, API responses)
 *
 * ## Operations
 *
 * **Arithmetic with date units:**
 * ```
 * val tomorrow = today.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.DAY)
 * val nextMonth = today.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.MONTH)
 * val nextYear = today.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.YEAR)
 * ```
 *
 * **Arithmetic with DatePeriod:**
 * ```
 * val future = date + DatePeriod(years = 1, months = 2, days = 3)
 * val period = date1.periodUntil(date2)
 * ```
 *
 * **Calculating differences:**
 * ```
 * val daysBetween = date1.daysUntil(date2)
 * val monthsBetween = date1.monthsUntil(date2)
 * val yearsBetween = date1.yearsUntil(date2)
 * ```
 *
 * **Combining with time:**
 * ```
 * val dateTime = date.atTime(14, 30)  // 14:30 on this date
 * val dateTime2 = date.atTime(LocalTime(14, 30, 0))
 * ```
 *
 * **Converting to Instant:**
 * ```
 * val startOfDay = date.atStartOfDayIn(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
 * ```
 *
 * Note: The arithmetic on [LocalDate] values is defined independently of the time zone (so `2020-08-30` plus one day
 * is `2020-08-31` everywhere): see various [LocalDate.plus] and [LocalDate.minus] functions, as well
 * as [LocalDate.periodUntil] and various other [*until][LocalDate.daysUntil] functions.
 *
 * @see LocalDateTime for date and time together
 * @see LocalTime for time of day
 * @see Instant for absolute moments in time
 * @see atStartOfDayIn for converting to an Instant
 */"}

{"file_path": "/project/core/common/src/LocalDateTime.kt", "old_string": "/**
 * The representation of a specific civil date and time without a reference to a particular time zone.
 *
 * This class does not describe specific *moments in time*, which are represented as [Instant] values.
 * Instead, its instances can be thought of as clock readings, something that an observer in a particular time zone
 * could witness.
 * For example, `2020-08-30T18:43` is not a *moment in time*, since someone in Berlin and someone in Tokyo would witness
 * this on their clocks at different times.
 *
 * The main purpose of this class is to provide human-readable representations of [Instant] values, or to transfer them
 * as data.
 *
 * The arithmetic on [LocalDateTime] values is not provided, since without accounting for the time zone transitions it may give misleading results.
 */", "new_string": "/**
 * The representation of a specific civil date and time without a reference to a particular time zone.
 *
 * This class does not describe specific *moments in time*, which are represented as [Instant] values.
 * Instead, its instances can be thought of as clock readings, something that an observer in a particular time zone
 * could witness.
 * For example, `2020-08-30T18:43` is not a *moment in time*, since someone in Berlin and someone in Tokyo would witness
 * this on their clocks at different times.
 *
 * ## ISO-8601 compliance
 *
 * [LocalDateTime] follows the ISO-8601 calendar system for dates (proleptic Gregorian calendar) and
 * the ISO-8601 time format (24-hour clock).
 *
 * ## Acquiring a LocalDateTime
 *
 * A [LocalDateTime] can be obtained in several ways:
 *
 * **From an Instant and a TimeZone:**
 * ```
 * val instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val localDateTime = instant.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
 * ```
 *
 * **By constructing from components:**
 * ```
 * val dateTime1 = LocalDateTime(2023, 4, 15, 14, 30)  // April 15, 2023, 14:30:00
 * val dateTime2 = LocalDateTime(2023, Month.APRIL, 15, 14, 30, 0)  // with seconds
 * val dateTime3 = LocalDateTime(2023, 4, 15, 14, 30, 0, 500_000_000)  // with nanoseconds
 * ```
 *
 * **By combining LocalDate and LocalTime:**
 * ```
 * val date = LocalDate(2023, 4, 15)
 * val time = LocalTime(14, 30)
 * val dateTime = LocalDateTime(date, time)
 * // or
 * val dateTime2 = date.atTime(14, 30)
 * val dateTime3 = time.atDate(date)
 * ```
 *
 * **By parsing an ISO-8601 string:**
 * ```
 * val dateTime = LocalDateTime.parse("2023-04-15T14:30:00")
 * ```
 *
 * ## Usage
 *
 * [LocalDateTime] is primarily used for:
 * - Displaying human-readable date and time information from an [Instant]
 * - Representing scheduled events in the far future (store with a [TimeZone] separately)
 * - Transferring date-time data where time zone information is handled separately
 *
 * **When to use LocalDateTime:**
 * - For far-future scheduled events (meetings, appointments) where time zone rules might change
 * - When you need to display an [Instant] to users in their local time zone
 * - When exchanging date-time data in a format independent of time zones
 *
 * **When NOT to use LocalDateTime:**
 * - For timestamps of past events (use [Instant] instead)
 * - For near-future events with well-defined moments (use [Instant] instead)
 * - For date-only or time-only values (use [LocalDate] or [LocalTime] instead)
 *
 * ## Converting to Instant
 *
 * To convert a [LocalDateTime] to an [Instant], you must specify a [TimeZone].
 * This conversion can be ambiguous due to daylight saving time transitions:
 *
 * ```
 * val localDateTime = LocalDateTime(2023, 3, 26, 2, 30)  // During DST transition in some zones
 * val instant = localDateTime.toInstant(TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
 * ```
 *
 * **Time gaps (spring forward):** When clocks move forward, some local times don't exist.
 * In this case, [toInstant] uses the offset from before the transition.
 *
 * **Time overlaps (fall back):** When clocks move backward, some local times occur twice.
 * In this case, [toInstant] returns the earlier instant.
 *
 * ## Why no arithmetic?
 *
 * Arithmetic on [LocalDateTime] is intentionally not provided because it would be misleading
 * without accounting for time zone transitions:
 *
 * - Adding 1 day to `2023-03-25T12:00` might or might not be 24 hours later, depending on DST
 * - Some date-times don't exist (time gaps) or occur twice (time overlaps)
 *
 * **Recommended approach:**
 * ```
 * val timeZone = TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin")
 * val localDateTime = LocalDateTime(2021, 3, 27, 2, 16, 20)
 * 
 * // Convert to Instant, do arithmetic, convert back
 * val instant = localDateTime.toInstant(timeZone)
 * val instantOneDayLater = instant.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.DAY, timeZone)
 * val localDateTimeOneDayLater = instantOneDayLater.toLocalDateTime(timeZone)
 * ```
 *
 * @see Instant for absolute moments in time
 * @see LocalDate for date without time
 * @see LocalTime for time without date
 * @see TimeZone for time zone rules
 */"}

{"file_path": "/project/core/common/src/LocalTime.kt", "old_string": "/**
 * The time part of [LocalDateTime].
 *
 * This class represents time-of-day without a referencing a specific date.
 * To reconstruct a full [LocalDateTime], representing civil date and time, [LocalTime] needs to be
 * combined with [LocalDate] via [LocalDate.atTime] or [LocalTime.atDate].
 *
 * Also, [LocalTime] does not reference a particular time zone.
 * Therefore, even on the same date, [LocalTime] denotes different moments of time.
 * For example, `18:43` happens at different moments in Berlin and in Tokyo.
 *
 * The arithmetic on [LocalTime] values is not provided, since without accounting for the time zone
 * transitions it may give misleading results.
 */", "new_string": "/**
 * The time part of [LocalDateTime].
 *
 * This class represents time-of-day without referencing a specific date.
 * To reconstruct a full [LocalDateTime], representing civil date and time, [LocalTime] needs to be
 * combined with [LocalDate] via [LocalDate.atTime] or [LocalTime.atDate].
 *
 * Also, [LocalTime] does not reference a particular time zone.
 * Therefore, even on the same date, [LocalTime] denotes different moments of time.
 * For example, `18:43` happens at different moments in Berlin and in Tokyo.
 *
 * ## ISO-8601 compliance
 *
 * [LocalTime] follows the ISO-8601 time format using a 24-hour clock.
 * Hours range from 0 to 23, minutes and seconds from 0 to 59, and nanoseconds from 0 to 999,999,999.
 *
 * ## Acquiring a LocalTime
 *
 * A [LocalTime] can be obtained in several ways:
 *
 * **From an Instant and TimeZone:**
 * ```
 * val instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val time = instant.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault()).time
 * ```
 *
 * **By constructing from components:**
 * ```
 * val time1 = LocalTime(14, 30)  // 14:30:00
 * val time2 = LocalTime(14, 30, 45)  // 14:30:45
 * val time3 = LocalTime(14, 30, 45, 500_000_000)  // with nanoseconds
 * ```
 *
 * **By parsing an ISO-8601 string:**
 * ```
 * val time = LocalTime.parse("14:30:00")
 * ```
 *
 * **From seconds, milliseconds, or nanoseconds of day:**
 * ```
 * val time1 = LocalTime.fromSecondOfDay(52200)  // 14:30:00
 * val time2 = LocalTime.fromMillisecondOfDay(52200000)
 * val time3 = LocalTime.fromNanosecondOfDay(52200000000000)
 * ```
 *
 * ## Usage
 *
 * [LocalTime] is ideal for representing:
 * - Time-of-day values independent of dates (e.g., business hours: "open from 09:00 to 17:00")
 * - Recurring daily events (e.g., "daily backup at 02:00")
 * - Durations within a single day
 *
 * **When to use LocalTime:**
 * - When the date is irrelevant or handled separately
 * - For time patterns that repeat daily
 * - When extracting just the time component from a [LocalDateTime]
 *
 * ## Operations
 *
 * **Combining with dates:**
 * ```
 * val date = LocalDate(2023, 4, 15)
 * val time = LocalTime(14, 30)
 * val dateTime1 = date.atTime(time)
 * val dateTime2 = time.atDate(date)
 * ```
 *
 * **Converting to/from day units:**
 * ```
 * val time = LocalTime(14, 30, 0)
 * val secondOfDay = time.toSecondOfDay()  // 52200
 * val millisOfDay = time.toMillisecondOfDay()  // 52200000
 * val nanosOfDay = time.toNanosecondOfDay()  // 52200000000000
 * ```
 *
 * **Comparing times:**
 * ```
 * val morning = LocalTime(9, 0)
 * val evening = LocalTime(17, 0)
 * val isWorkingHours = currentTime in morning..evening
 * ```
 *
 * ## Why no arithmetic?
 *
 * Arithmetic on [LocalTime] is not provided because:
 * - Time arithmetic without dates can be ambiguous (e.g., adding 1 hour to 23:30)
 * - Without time zone information, results during DST transitions would be misleading
 * - Day boundaries would need special handling
 *
 * For time arithmetic, convert to [LocalDateTime] or [Instant] first.
 *
 * ## Note on comparison
 *
 * While [LocalTime] values can be compared (earlier vs. later in a day), be aware that on days with
 * time overlaps (e.g., autumn DST transitions), a "lesser" time value might actually occur after a
 * "greater" one chronologically.
 *
 * @see LocalDateTime for date and time together
 * @see LocalDate for date without time
 * @see Instant for absolute moments in time
 */"}

{"file_path": "/project/core/common/src/TimeZone.kt", "old_string": "/**
 * A time zone, provides the conversion between [Instant] and [LocalDateTime] values
 * using a collection of rules specifying which [LocalDateTime] value corresponds to each [Instant].
 */", "new_string": "/**
 * A time zone, provides the conversion between [Instant] and [LocalDateTime] values
 * using a collection of rules specifying which [LocalDateTime] value corresponds to each [Instant].
 *
 * Time zones account for various regional date-time regulations, such as daylight saving time (DST)
 * transitions, historical offset changes, and more.
 *
 * ## Acquiring a TimeZone
 *
 * A [TimeZone] can be obtained in several ways:
 *
 * **The system's current time zone:**
 * ```
 * val systemZone = TimeZone.currentSystemDefault()
 * ```
 *
 * **UTC time zone:**
 * ```
 * val utc = TimeZone.UTC
 * ```
 *
 * **By identifier (IANA Time Zone Database names):**
 * ```
 * val berlin = TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin")
 * val newYork = TimeZone.of("America/New_York")
 * val tokyo = TimeZone.of("Asia/Tokyo")
 * ```
 *
 * **Fixed offset time zones:**
 * ```
 * val utcPlus3 = TimeZone.of("UTC+3")
 * val fixedOffset = FixedOffsetTimeZone(UtcOffset(hours = 5, minutes = 30))
 * ```
 *
 * **Available time zones:**
 * ```
 * val allZoneIds = TimeZone.availableZoneIds
 * ```
 *
 * ## ISO-8601 and IANA Time Zone Database
 *
 * Time zone identifiers follow the IANA Time Zone Database (TZDB) standard, typically in the format
 * `Area/Location` (e.g., `Europe/Berlin`, `America/New_York`). The library also supports ISO-8601
 * fixed offset formats (e.g., `+03:00`, `-05:30`).
 *
 * ## Usage
 *
 * **Converting Instant to LocalDateTime:**
 * ```
 * val instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val localDateTime = instant.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
 * ```
 *
 * **Converting LocalDateTime to Instant:**
 * ```
 * val localDateTime = LocalDateTime(2023, 3, 26, 2, 30)
 * val instant = localDateTime.toInstant(TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
 * ```
 *
 * **Finding the offset at a specific instant:**
 * ```
 * val instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val offset = instant.offsetIn(TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
 * println("Current offset: $offset")  // e.g., +01:00 or +02:00
 * ```
 *
 * **Getting the current date in a time zone:**
 * ```
 * val today = Clock.System.todayIn(TimeZone.of("Asia/Tokyo"))
 * ```
 *
 * ## Time zone transitions and ambiguities
 *
 * Time zones can have transitions (typically for daylight saving time) that create two types of
 * ambiguous situations:
 *
 * **Time gaps (spring forward):** When clocks move forward, some local date-times don't exist.
 * For example, if clocks jump from 02:00 to 03:00, the local time 02:30 never occurs.
 * When converting such a [LocalDateTime] to an [Instant] using [LocalDateTime.toInstant],
 * the offset from *before* the transition is used.
 *
 * **Time overlaps (fall back):** When clocks move backward, some local date-times occur twice.
 * For example, if clocks fall back from 03:00 to 02:00, the local time 02:30 occurs twice.
 * When converting such a [LocalDateTime] to an [Instant] using [LocalDateTime.toInstant],
 * the *earlier* instant is returned.
 *
 * Example:
 * ```
 * val berlin = TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin")
 * 
 * // During DST transition (gap)
 * val gapTime = LocalDateTime(2023, 3, 26, 2, 30)  // This time doesn't exist
 * val instant1 = gapTime.toInstant(berlin)  // Resolved using pre-transition offset
 * 
 * // During fall-back transition (overlap)
 * val overlapTime = LocalDateTime(2023, 10, 29, 2, 30)  // This time occurs twice
 * val instant2 = overlapTime.toInstant(berlin)  // Returns the earlier occurrence
 * ```
 *
 * ## System behavior
 *
 * [TimeZone.currentSystemDefault] queries the system's time zone setting. If the system time zone
 * changes while the application is running, subsequent calls may return a different [TimeZone].
 *
 * ## Fixed offset time zones
 *
 * For time zones that never change their offset from UTC, use [FixedOffsetTimeZone]:
 * ```
 * val offset = UtcOffset(hours = 5, minutes = 30)
 * val fixedZone = FixedOffsetTimeZone(offset)
 * ```
 *
 * @see Instant for absolute moments in time
 * @see LocalDateTime for date-time without a time zone
 * @see UtcOffset for representing offsets from UTC
 * @see FixedOffsetTimeZone for time zones with constant offsets
 */"}

 * A time zone that is known to always have the same offset from UTC.

 * Unlike region-based time zones (like `Europe/Berlin`), fixed offset time zones never have
 * daylight saving time transitions or any other offset changes.
 *
 * ## Acquiring a FixedOffsetTimeZone

 * ```
 * val offset = UtcOffset(hours = 5, minutes = 30)
 * val fixedZone = FixedOffsetTimeZone(offset)
 * ```
 *

 * ```
 * val utcPlus3 = TimeZone.of("UTC+3")
 * val utcMinus5 = TimeZone.of("-05:00")
 * ```
 *
 * **The UTC time zone:**
 * ```
 * val utc = TimeZone.UTC
 * ```
 *
 * ## Usage
 *
 * Fixed offset time zones are useful when:
 * - Working with data that specifies an explicit offset (e.g., `2023-04-15T14:30:00+05:30`)
 * - You need consistent offset behavior without DST transitions
 * - Interfacing with systems that use fixed offsets rather than named time zones
 *
 * Example:
 * ```
 * val offset = UtcOffset(hours = 3)
 * val fixedZone = FixedOffsetTimeZone(offset)
 * 
 * val instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val localDateTime = instant.toLocalDateTime(fixedZone)
 * ```
 *
 * @see TimeZone for general time zone operations
 * @see UtcOffset for representing offsets from UTC
 */"}

{"file_path": "/project/core/common/src/UtcOffset.kt", "old_string": "/**
 * An offset from UTC.
 *
 * Examples of these values:
 * - `Z`, an offset of zero;
 * - `+05`, plus five hours;
 * - `-02`, minus two hours;
 * - `+03:30`, plus three hours and thirty minutes;
 * - `+01:23:45`, plus one hour, 23 minutes, and 45 seconds.
 */", "new_string": "/**
 * An offset from UTC.
 *
 * A [UtcOffset] represents the difference between local time and UTC (Coordinated Universal Time).
 * Positive offsets are east of UTC (ahead in time), negative offsets are west of UTC (behind in time).
 *
 * ## ISO-8601 compliance
 *
 * [UtcOffset] follows the ISO-8601 format for UTC offsets. Examples:
 * - `Z`, an offset of zero (UTC itself)
 * - `+05` or `+05:00`, plus five hours (e.g., Pakistan Standard Time)
 * - `-02` or `-02:00`, minus two hours
 * - `+03:30`, plus three hours and thirty minutes (e.g., Iran Standard Time)
 * - `+05:45`, plus five hours and forty-five minutes (e.g., Nepal Time)
 * - `+01:23:45`, plus one hour, 23 minutes, and 45 seconds (rare but supported)
 *
 * ## Acquiring a UtcOffset
 *
 * **Zero offset (UTC):**
 * ```
 * val zero = UtcOffset.ZERO
 * ```
 *
 * **By constructing from components:**
 * ```
 * val offset1 = UtcOffset(hours = 5)  // +05:00
 * val offset2 = UtcOffset(hours = 5, minutes = 30)  // +05:30
 * val offset3 = UtcOffset(hours = -3)  // -03:00
 * val offset4 = UtcOffset(hours = 1, minutes = 23, seconds = 45)  // +01:23:45
 * ```
 *
 * **By parsing an ISO-8601 string:**
 * ```
 * val offset = UtcOffset.parse("+05:30")
 * ```
 *
 * **From a TimeZone at a specific Instant:**
 * ```
 * val instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val offset = instant.offsetIn(TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
 * ```
 *
 * ## Usage
 *
 * [UtcOffset] is primarily used for:
 * - Creating [FixedOffsetTimeZone] instances
 * - Representing the offset component of date-time values
 * - Converting between [Instant] and [LocalDateTime] with a known offset
 * - Working with ISO-8601 formatted date-times that include an offset
 *
 * **Converting with explicit offset:**
 * ```
 * val offset = UtcOffset(hours = 3)
 * val instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val localDateTime = instant.toLocalDateTime(offset)
 * val backToInstant = localDateTime.toInstant(offset)
 * ```
 *
 * **Creating a fixed offset time zone:**
 * ```
 * val offset = UtcOffset(hours = 5, minutes = 30)
 * val timeZone = offset.asTimeZone()
 * // or
 * val timeZone2 = FixedOffsetTimeZone(offset)
 * ```
 *
 * ## Total seconds representation
 *
 * Internally, offsets are stored as [totalSeconds] - the number of seconds by which the local
 * time is ahead of UTC. Larger values mean the local time is further east (earlier in absolute time).
 *
 * For example:
 * - `+05:30` has `totalSeconds = 19800` (5*3600 + 30*60)
 * - `-03:00` has `totalSeconds = -10800` (-3*3600)
 *
 * ## Valid range
 *
 * [UtcOffset] values must be in the range of Â±18:00 (Â±64800 seconds). This is the maximum offset
 * used by any inhabited time zone.
 *
 * @see TimeZone for converting between Instant and LocalDateTime
 * @see FixedOffsetTimeZone for time zones with constant offsets
 * @see Instant.offsetIn for finding the offset of a time zone at a specific instant
 */"}

{"file_path": "/project/core/common/src/DateTimeUnit.kt", "old_string": "/**
 * A unit for measuring time.
 *
 * See the predefined constants for time units, like [DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND], [DateTimeUnit.DAY],
 * [DateTimeUnit.MONTH], and others.
 *
 * Two ways are provided to create custom [DateTimeUnit] instances:
 * - By multiplying an existing unit on the right by an integer scalar: for example, `DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND * 10`.
 * - By constructing an instance manually with [TimeBased], [DayBased], or [MonthBased]: for example,
 *   `TimeBased(nanoseconds = 10)`.
 *
 * Note that a calendar day is not considered identical to 24 hours. See [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for a discussion.
 */", "new_string": "/**
 * A unit for measuring time.
 *
 * [DateTimeUnit] represents various units of time used in date and time arithmetic, from nanoseconds
 * to centuries. Units are categorized into three types based on their properties:
 * - [TimeBased]: precise duration units (nanoseconds to hours)
 * - [DayBased]: calendar day units (days, weeks)
 * - [MonthBased]: calendar month units (months, quarters, years, centuries)
 *
 * ## Predefined units
 *
 * **Time-based units (fixed duration):**
 * ```
 * DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND   // 1 nanosecond
 * DateTimeUnit.MICROSECOND  // 1000 nanoseconds
 * DateTimeUnit.MILLISECOND  // 1,000,000 nanoseconds
 * DateTimeUnit.SECOND       // 1,000,000,000 nanoseconds
 * DateTimeUnit.MINUTE       // 60 seconds
 * DateTimeUnit.HOUR         // 60 minutes, 3600 seconds
 * ```
 *
 * **Day-based units (calendar days):**
 * ```
 * DateTimeUnit.DAY          // 1 calendar day
 * DateTimeUnit.WEEK         // 7 calendar days
 * ```
 *
 * **Month-based units (calendar months):**
 * ```
 * DateTimeUnit.MONTH        // 1 calendar month
 * DateTimeUnit.QUARTER      // 3 months
 * DateTimeUnit.YEAR         // 12 months
 * DateTimeUnit.CENTURY      // 100 years, 1200 months
 * ```
 *
 * ## Creating custom units
 *
 * **By multiplication:**
 * ```
 * val twoWeeks = DateTimeUnit.WEEK * 2
 * val fiveMinutes = DateTimeUnit.MINUTE * 5
 * val threeMonths = DateTimeUnit.MONTH * 3
 * ```
 *
 * **By construction:**
 * ```
 * val customTime = DateTimeUnit.TimeBased(nanoseconds = 10_000_000)
 * val customDays = DateTimeUnit.DayBased(days = 14)
 * val customMonths = DateTimeUnit.MonthBased(months = 6)
 * ```
 *
 * ## Usage with Instant
 *
 * **Time-based units (no TimeZone needed):**
 * ```
 * val instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val later = instant.plus(5, DateTimeUnit.HOUR)
 * val earlier = instant.minus(30, DateTimeUnit.MINUTE)
 * val hoursBetween = instant1.until(instant2, DateTimeUnit.HOUR)
 * ```
 *
 * **Date-based units (TimeZone required):**
 * ```
 * val instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val timeZone = TimeZone.currentSystemDefault()
 * 
 * val tomorrow = instant.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.DAY, timeZone)
 * val nextMonth = instant.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.MONTH, timeZone)
 * val daysBetween = instant1.daysUntil(instant2, timeZone)
 * ```
 *
 * ## Usage with LocalDate
 *
 * ```
 * val date = LocalDate(2023, 1, 15)
 * val nextWeek = date.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.WEEK)
 * val nextYear = date.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.YEAR)
 * val monthsBetween = date1.monthsUntil(date2)
 * ```
 *
 * ## Important distinction: Calendar days vs. 24-hour periods
 *
 * A calendar [DAY] is **not** the same as 24 hours due to daylight saving time transitions:
 * - Some days are 23 hours (spring forward)
 * - Some days are 25 hours (fall back)
 * - Most days are 24 hours
 *
 * Example:
 * ```
 * val instant = Instant.parse("2023-03-25T12:00:00Z")
 * val timeZone = TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin")
 * 
 * // Adding 1 calendar DAY (may not be 24 hours due to DST)
 * val oneDayLater = instant.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.DAY, timeZone)
 * 
 * // Adding 24 hours (exactly 24 hours, ignores DST)
 * val twentyFourHoursLater = instant.plus(24, DateTimeUnit.HOUR)
 * 
 * // These may be different!
 * ```
 *
 * This is why:
 * - [TimeBased] units don't require a [TimeZone] (they represent fixed durations)
 * - [DayBased] and [MonthBased] units require a [TimeZone] when used with [Instant]
 *
 * @see Instant.plus
 * @see Instant.until
 * @see LocalDate.plus
 * @see LocalDate.until
 */"}

{"file_path": "/project/core/common/src/DateTimePeriod.kt", "old_string": "/**
 * A difference between two [instants][Instant], decomposed into date and time components.
 *
 * The date components are: [years], [months], [days].
 *
 * The time components are: [hours], [minutes], [seconds], [nanoseconds].
 *
 * A `DateTimePeriod` can be constructed using the same-named constructor function,
 * [parsed][DateTimePeriod.parse] from a string, or returned as the result of instant arithmetic operations (see [Instant.periodUntil]).
 * All these functions can return a [DatePeriod] value, which is a subtype of `DateTimePeriod`,
 * a special case that only stores date components, if all time components of the result happen to be zero.
 */", "new_string": "/**
 * A difference between two [instants][Instant], decomposed into date and time components.
 *
 * The date components are: [years], [months], [days].
 *
 * The time components are: [hours], [minutes], [seconds], [nanoseconds].
 *
 * ## ISO-8601 compliance
 *
 * [DateTimePeriod] uses ISO-8601 duration format for string representation. Examples:
 * - `P1Y2M3D` - 1 year, 2 months, 3 days
 * - `PT5H30M` - 5 hours and 30 minutes
 * - `P1DT12H` - 1 day and 12 hours
 * - `P1Y2M3DT4H5M6.007S` - 1 year, 2 months, 3 days, 4 hours, 5 minutes, 6 seconds, and 7 milliseconds
 * - `-P1D` - negative: minus 1 day
 *
 * Additionally, this library supports the `W` designator for weeks: `P2W` equals `P14D`.
 *
 * ## Acquiring a DateTimePeriod
 *
 * **By construction:**
 * ```
 * val period1 = DateTimePeriod(years = 1, months = 2, days = 3)
 * val period2 = DateTimePeriod(hours = 5, minutes = 30)
 * val period3 = DateTimePeriod(days = 1, hours = 12, minutes = 30)
 * ```
 *
 * **From Duration:**
 * ```
 * val duration = 5.hours + 30.minutes
 * val period = duration.toDateTimePeriod()
 * ```
 *
 * **From the difference between two instants:**
 * ```
 * val instant1 = Instant.parse("2023-01-01T00:00:00Z")
 * val instant2 = Instant.parse("2024-03-15T14:30:00Z")
 * val period = instant1.periodUntil(instant2, TimeZone.UTC)
 * ```
 *
 * **By parsing an ISO-8601 duration string:**
 * ```
 * val period = DateTimePeriod.parse("P1Y2M3DT4H5M6S")
 * ```
 *
 * ## Usage
 *
 * [DateTimePeriod] is used for:
 * - Representing the difference between two instants in human-readable calendar units
 * - Adding or subtracting calendar-based periods from instants
 * - Expressing complex time spans that mix date and time components
 *
 * **Adding to an Instant:**
 * ```
 * val instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val period = DateTimePeriod(months = 1, days = 15, hours = 3)
 * val later = instant.plus(period, TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
 * ```
 *
 * **Calculating the period between instants:**
 * ```
 * val start = Instant.parse("2023-01-01T00:00:00Z")
 * val end = Instant.parse("2023-03-15T14:30:00Z")
 * val period = start.periodUntil(end, TimeZone.UTC)
 * println("${period.months} months and ${period.days} days")
 * ```
 *
 * **Combining periods:**
 * ```
 * val period1 = DateTimePeriod(months = 1)
 * val period2 = DateTimePeriod(days = 15)
 * val combined = period1 + period2
 * ```
 *
 * ## DatePeriod subtype
 *
 * If all time components are zero, constructor functions automatically return [DatePeriod],
 * a subtype of [DateTimePeriod]:
 * ```
 * val period = DateTimePeriod(years = 1, months = 2, days = 3)
 * // This is actually a DatePeriod instance
 * val datePeriod = period as? DatePeriod  // succeeds
 * ```
 *
 * [DatePeriod] is specifically designed for use with [LocalDate] and represents date-only periods.
 *
 * ## Component normalization
 *
 * Components are normalized for human readability:
 * ```
 * val period = DateTimePeriod(months = 24)
 * println(period)  // prints "P2Y" (2 years), not "P24M"
 * ```
 *
 * ## Important notes
 *
 * - When adding a [DateTimePeriod] to an [Instant], components are added in order from largest to smallest
 *   (years â†’ months â†’ days â†’ hours â†’ minutes â†’ seconds â†’ nanoseconds)
 * - The [TimeZone] is required because calendar arithmetic depends on time zone rules (DST, etc.)
 * - Negative components are supported: `DateTimePeriod(days = -1)` represents "minus one day"
 *
 * @see DatePeriod for date-only periods
 * @see Instant.periodUntil for calculating periods
 * @see Instant.plus for adding periods to instants
 * @see Duration for fixed-length time spans
 */"}

 */", "new_string": "/**
 * A special case of [DateTimePeriod] that only stores date components and has all time components equal to zero.
 *
 * A [DatePeriod] is automatically returned from all constructor functions for [DateTimePeriod] if it turns out that
 * the time components are zero.
 *
 * [DatePeriod] values are used in operations on [LocalDates][LocalDate] and are returned from operations on [LocalDates][LocalDate],
 * but they also can be passed anywhere a [DateTimePeriod] is expected.
 *
 * ## ISO-8601 compliance
 *
 * [DatePeriod] uses the date portion of ISO-8601 duration format. Examples:
 * - `P1Y` - 1 year
 * - `P2M` - 2 months
 * - `P10D` - 10 days
 * - `P1Y2M3D` - 1 year, 2 months, and 3 days
 * - `P2W` - 2 weeks (equals `P14D`)
 *
 * ## Acquiring a DatePeriod
 *
 * **By construction:**
 * ```
 * val period1 = DatePeriod(years = 1, months = 2, days = 3)
 * val period2 = DatePeriod(months = 6)
 * val period3 = DatePeriod(days = 30)
 * ```
 *
 * **From the difference between two LocalDates:**
 * ```
 * val date1 = LocalDate(2023, 1, 1)
 * val date2 = LocalDate(2024, 3, 15)
 * val period = date1.periodUntil(date2)
 * // or
 * val period2 = date2 - date1
 * ```
 *
 * **By parsing an ISO-8601 duration string:**
 * ```
 * val period = DatePeriod.parse("P1Y2M3D")
 * ```
 *
 * **Automatically from DateTimePeriod:**
 * ```
 * val period = DateTimePeriod(years = 1, months = 2, days = 3)
 * // This is actually a DatePeriod because time components are zero
 * ```
 *
 * ## Usage
 *
 * [DatePeriod] is specifically designed for calendar date arithmetic:
 *
 * **Adding to LocalDate:**
 * ```
 * val date = LocalDate(2023, 1, 15)
 * val period = DatePeriod(months = 1, days = 10)
 * val later = date + period  // 2023-02-25
 * ```
 *
 * **Subtracting from LocalDate:**
 * ```
 * val date = LocalDate(2023, 3, 15)
 * val period = DatePeriod(months = 1)
 * val earlier = date - period  // 2023-02-15
 * ```
 *
 * **Calculating the period between dates:**
 * ```
 * val start = LocalDate(2023, 1, 1)
 * val end = LocalDate(2024, 3, 15)
 * val period = start.periodUntil(end)
 * println("${period.years} years, ${period.months} months, ${period.days} days")
 * ```
 *
 * **Combining periods:**
 * ```
 * val period1 = DatePeriod(years = 1)
 * val period2 = DatePeriod(months = 6)
 * val combined = period1 + period2  // P1Y6M
 * ```
 *
 * ## Interoperability with DateTimePeriod
 *
 * [DatePeriod] can be used anywhere [DateTimePeriod] is expected:
 * ```
 * val datePeriod = DatePeriod(months = 1, days = 15)
 * val instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val later = instant.plus(datePeriod, TimeZone.UTC)  // Works!
 * ```
 *
 * ## Component normalization
 *
 * Like [DateTimePeriod], components are normalized for readability:
 * ```
 * val period = DatePeriod(months = 24)
 * println(period)  // prints "P2Y", not "P24M"
 * ```
 *
 * @see DateTimePeriod for periods with both date and time components
 * @see LocalDate.periodUntil for calculating date periods
 * @see LocalDate.plus for adding periods to dates
 */"}

{"file_path": "/project/core/common/src/Exceptions.kt", "old_string": "/**
 * Thrown by date-time arithmetic operations if the result can not be computed or represented.
 */
public class DateTimeArithmeticException: RuntimeException {
    public constructor(): super()
    public constructor(message: String): super(message)
    public constructor(cause: Throwable): super(cause)
    public constructor(message: String, cause: Throwable): super(message, cause)
}

/**
 * Thrown when attempting to construct a [TimeZone] with an invalid ID.
 */
public class IllegalTimeZoneException: IllegalArgumentException {
    public constructor(): super()
    public constructor(message: String): super(message)
    public constructor(cause: Throwable): super(cause)
    public constructor(message: String, cause: Throwable): super(message, cause)
}", "new_string": "/**
 * Thrown by date-time arithmetic operations if the result cannot be computed or represented.
 *
 * This exception typically occurs in the following scenarios:
 *
 * ## Arithmetic overflow
 *
 * When an arithmetic operation produces a result that exceeds the boundaries of the type:
 * ```
 * val date = LocalDate.MAX
 * val tomorrow = date.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.DAY)  // throws DateTimeArithmeticException
 * ```
 *
 * ## Invalid intermediate results
 *
 * When intermediate computation steps exceed type boundaries, even if the final result would be valid:
 * ```
 * val instant = Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE
 * val period = DateTimePeriod(years = 1000)
 * val result = instant.plus(period, timeZone)  // may throw if intermediate steps overflow
 * ```
 *
 * ## Platform-specific limits (JVM-specific)
 *
 * On the JVM, some operations have additional constraints. For example, calculating the number of months
 * between two dates that exceeds [Int.MAX_VALUE] will throw this exception.
 *
 * ## Handling this exception
 *
 * To avoid this exception:
 * - Check boundaries before performing arithmetic operations
 * - Use [Instant.DISTANT_PAST] and [Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE] as safe representable boundaries
 * - Be mindful of platform-specific limits when working with very large date ranges
 *
 * Example:
 * ```
 * val instant = Clock.System.now()
 * if (instant < Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE) {
 *     val later = instant.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.YEAR, timeZone)  // safe
 * }
 * ```
 *
 * @see Instant.DISTANT_PAST
 * @see Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE
 */
public class DateTimeArithmeticException: RuntimeException {
    public constructor(): super()
    public constructor(message: String): super(message)
    public constructor(cause: Throwable): super(cause)
    public constructor(message: String, cause: Throwable): super(message, cause)
}

/**
 * Thrown when attempting to construct a [TimeZone] with an invalid ID.
 *
 * This exception occurs when:
 *
 * ## Invalid time zone identifier format
 *
 * The time zone ID doesn't match any supported format:
 * ```
 * val zone = TimeZone.of("Invalid/Zone")  // throws IllegalTimeZoneException
 * ```
 *
 * ## Unknown time zone identifier
 *
 * The time zone ID is well-formed but not found in the time zone database:
 * ```
 * val zone = TimeZone.of("Europe/Atlantis")  // throws IllegalTimeZoneException
 * ```
 *
 * ## Invalid offset format
 *
 * When using fixed offset identifiers with incorrect syntax:
 * ```
 * val zone = TimeZone.of("+25:00")  // throws IllegalTimeZoneException (offset too large)
 * val zone2 = TimeZone.of("UTC+")   // throws IllegalTimeZoneException (incomplete)
 * ```
 *
 * ## Supported time zone identifier formats
 *
 * Valid formats include:
 * - `Z`, `UTC`, `UT`, `GMT` - UTC time zone
 * - `+HH:mm`, `-HH:mm`, `UTC+HH:mm`, etc. - fixed offset zones
 * - `Area/Location` - IANA time zone database identifiers (e.g., `Europe/Berlin`, `America/New_York`)
 *
 * ## How to avoid this exception
 *
 * **Check available zones:**
 * ```
 * val zoneId = "Europe/Berlin"
 * if (zoneId in TimeZone.availableZoneIds) {
 *     val zone = TimeZone.of(zoneId)
 * }
 * ```
 *
 * **Use well-known zones:**
 * ```
 * val utc = TimeZone.UTC  // always valid
 * val system = TimeZone.currentSystemDefault()  // always valid
 * ```
 *
 * **Validate user input:**
 * ```
 * fun getTimeZone(id: String): TimeZone? {
 *     return try {
 *         TimeZone.of(id)
 *     } catch (e: IllegalTimeZoneException) {
 *         null  // or provide a default
 *     }
 * }
 * ```
 *
 * @see TimeZone.of
 * @see TimeZone.availableZoneIds
 */
public class IllegalTimeZoneException: IllegalArgumentException {
    public constructor(): super()
    public constructor(message: String): super(message)
    public constructor(cause: Throwable): super(cause)
    public constructor(message: String, cause: Throwable): super(message, cause)
}"}

10â†’/**
11â†’ * A source of [Instant] values.
12â†’ *
13â†’ * The primary purpose of [Clock] is to provide access to the current moment in time.
14â†’ * The most common implementation is [Clock.System], which queries the operating system's clock.
15â†’ *
16â†’ * ## Acquiring the current time
17â†’ *
18â†’ * To obtain the current moment in time as an [Instant]:
19â†’ * ```
20â†’ * val now = Clock.System.now()
21â†’ * ```
22â†’ *
23â†’ * To obtain the current date in a specific time zone:
24â†’ * ```
25â†’ * val today = Clock.System.todayIn(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
26â†’ * ```
27â†’ *
28â†’ * ## Using Clock for testability
29â†’ *
30â†’ * The [Clock] interface is designed to be injectable, making it easy to test time-dependent code.
31â†’ * Instead of directly calling [Clock.System], you can pass a [Clock] instance as a parameter:
32â†’ *
33â†’ * ```
34â†’ * class EventLogger(private val clock: Clock) {
35â†’ *     fun logEvent(message: String) {
36â†’ *         val timestamp = clock.now()
37â†’ *         println("[$timestamp] $message")
38â†’ *     }
39â†’ * }
40â†’ * ```
41â†’ *
42â†’ * In production code, pass [Clock.System], and in tests, provide a test double that returns
43â†’ * predetermined [Instant] values.
44â†’ *
45â†’ * ## System clock behavior
46â†’ *
47â†’ * [Clock.System] reflects the system clock, which means:
48â†’ * - The returned [Instant] represents time on the UTC-SLS time scale (smeared leap seconds).
49â†’ * - The precision depends on the platform but is at least millisecond-level.
50â†’ * - The clock can jump backward or forward if the system time is adjusted (e.g., by NTP synchronization

1â†’/*

 6â†’package kotlinx.datetime
 7â†’
 8â†’/**
 9â†’ * The enumeration class representing the days of the week.
10â†’ *
11â†’ * This enum follows the ISO-8601 standard, where Monday is the first day of the week (day 1)
12â†’ * and Sunday is the last day (day 7).
13â†’ *
14â†’ * ## Acquiring a DayOfWeek
15â†’ *
16â†’ * A [DayOfWeek] value can be obtained in several ways:
17â†’ * - From a [LocalDate] via its [LocalDate.dayOfWeek] property
18â†’ * - From a [LocalDateTime] via its [LocalDateTime.dayOfWeek] property
19â†’ * - By constructing from an ISO day number using [DayOfWeek] function: `DayOfWeek(1)` returns [MONDAY]
20â†’ * - Directly using the enum constants: [MONDAY], [TUESDAY], etc.
21â†’ *
22â†’ * ## ISO-8601 compliance
23â†’ *
24â†’ * The [isoDayNumber] property returns the ISO-8601 day-of-week number:
25â†’ * - [MONDAY] = 1
26â†’ * - [TUESDAY] = 2
27â†’ * - [WEDNESDAY] = 3
28â†’ * - [THURSDAY] = 4
29â†’ * - [FRIDAY] = 5
30â†’ * - [SATURDAY] = 6
31â†’ * - [SUNDAY] = 7
32â†’ *
33â†’ * ## Usage examples
34â†’ *
35â†’ * Checking if a date falls on a weekend:
36â†’ * ```
37â†’ * val date = LocalDate(2024, 1, 15)
38â†’ * val isWeekend = date.dayOfWeek in setOf(DayOfWeek.SATURDAY, DayOfWeek.SUNDAY)
39â†’ * ```
40â†’ *
41â†’ * Getting the day of week from an instant:
42â†’ * ```
43â†’ * val instant = Clock.System.now()
44â†’ * val dayOfWeek = instant.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault()).dayOfWeek
45â†’ * println("Today is $dayOfWeek")
46â†’ * ```
47â†’ *
48â†’ * Constructing from ISO day number:
49â†’ * ```
50â†’ * val monday = DayOfWeek(1)  // MONDAY
51â†’ * val sunday = DayOfWeek(7)  // SUNDAY
52â†’ * ```
53â†’ *
54â†’ * @see LocalDate.dayOfWeek
55â†’ * @see LocalDateTime.dayOfWeek
56â†’ * @see isoDayNumber
57â†’ */
58â†’public expect enum class DayOfWeek {
59â†’    /** Monday, the first day of the week according to ISO-8601. */
60â†’    MONDAY,
61â†’    
62â†’    /** Tuesday, the second day of the week according to ISO-8601. */
63â†’    TUESDAY,
64â†’    
65â†’    /** Wednesday, the third day of the week according to ISO-8601. */
66â†’    WEDNESDAY,
67â†’    
68â†’    /** Thursday, the fourth day of the week according to ISO-8601. */
69â†’    THURSDAY,
70â†’    
71â†’    /** Friday, the fifth day of the week according to ISO-8601. */
72â†’    FRIDAY,
73â†’    
74â†’    /** Saturday, the sixth day of the week according to ISO-8601. */
75â†’    SATURDAY,
76â†’    
77â†’    /** Sunday, the seventh day of the week according to ISO-8601. */
78â†’    SUNDAY;
79â†’}
80â†’
81â†’/**
82â†’ * The ISO-8601 number of the given day of the week. Monday is 1, Sunday is 7.
83â†’ */
84â†’public val DayOfWeek.isoDayNumber: Int get() = ordinal + 1
85â†’
86â†’/**
87â†’ * Returns the [DayOfWeek] instance for the given ISO-8601 week day number. Monday is 1, Sunday is 7.
88â†’ */
89â†’public fun DayOfWeek(isoDayNumber: Int): DayOfWeek {
90â†’    require(isoDayNumber in 1..7) { "Expected ISO day-of-week number in 1..7, got $isoDayNumber" }
91â†’    return DayOfWeek.entries[isoDayNumber - 1]
92â†’}
